issue_title: Timezones are ignored when converting datetimes
issue_body:
The [API doc](https://developers.google.com/maps/documentation/directions/intro) specifies that departure/arrival times are in seconds since January 1, 1970 UTC. When generating the appropriate timestamp  from a Python `datetime` object in  [`convert.time`](https://github.com/googlemaps/google-maps-services-python/blob/master/googlemaps/convert.py#L188) the library ignores attached timezones, assuming local `datetime`s.

For example the following code returns wrong traffic information:
```
    gmaps = googlemaps.Client(key="XXX", client_id="XXX", client_secret="XXX")
    times = [
        datetime(2017, 9, 18, 8, 0, tzinfo=timezone.utc),
        datetime(2017, 9, 18, 9, 0, tzinfo=timezone(timedelta(hours=1))),
        datetime(2017, 9, 18, 8, 0, tzinfo=timezone(timedelta(hours=1)))
    ]
    for time in times:
        res = gmaps.directions('Montauban', 'Toulouse', departure_time=time)
        print('datetime: {} - duration: {}'.format(time, res[0]['legs'][0]['duration_in_traffic']['text']))
```

, where the first two times correspond to the exact same timestamp and the third time is an hour before the other two, print the following:

```
datetime: 2017-09-18 08:00:00+00:00 - duration: 55 mins
datetime: 2017-09-18 09:00:00+01:00 - duration: 50 mins
datetime: 2017-09-18 08:00:00+01:00 - duration: 55 mins
```

I think that the right fix would be to use the timezone aware `timestamp` datetime method rather than `timetuple` in `convert.time`. Was there a reason for using timetuple?

I can open a PR for that if it helps.

issue_title: Research DB corruption + new dbrecover command
issue_labels: [{'id': 493379738, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWw0OTMzNzk3Mzg=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/learningequality/kolibri/labels/DOCS', 'name': 'DOCS', 'color': 'c2e0c6', 'default': False}, {'id': 667034570, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWw2NjcwMzQ1NzA=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/learningequality/kolibri/labels/P0%20-%20critical', 'name': 'P0 - critical', 'color': 'ff6600', 'default': False}, {'id': 447172036, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWw0NDcxNzIwMzY=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/learningequality/kolibri/labels/TASK:%20bug%20fix', 'name': 'TASK: bug fix', 'color': '72486f', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
### Observed behavior

Perhaps because of older SQLite pragma settings (which are non-reversible btw), we are running into a lot of database corruptions ATM. Other probably causes are unclean shutdowns or migrations, or concurrent access (Iceqube?).

One aspect of this issue is to research why, the other is to find a remedy that's better than to leave the user with an application crash and a traceback.

1. [x] Research if possible to create a command `dbrecover` that essentially does `dbbackup` and `dbrestore --latest`
1. [x] Catch exception at appropriate place and show suggestion to ~use `dbrecover`~ (or `sqlite3 <path> ".dump" | sqlite3 <path>`)
1. [ ] Find out why we have these somewhat frequent errors.

### Expected behavior

Yes, the database can be crashed / malformed. We can never entirely rule that out.

### User-facing consequences

A user may decide to delete their entire database to get Kolibri running. Or might have to spend time on the internet. Or doesn't have internet, so will never solve the problem. It's pretty bad.

### Errors and logs

```
File “/usr/local/lib/python3.5/dist-packages/kolibri/dist/django/db/backends/sqlite3/base.py”, line 323, in execute
return Database.Cursor.execute(self, query, params)
django.db.utils.DatabaseError: database disk image is malformed
```

### Steps to reproduce

@radinamatic has tried it while running out of disk space, I have tried it during an upgrade from 0.7.1 to 0.7.2

Let's post some broken databases.

### Context

Well, any OS and possibly any version of Kolibri.

issue_title: suggestion: remove 'content type' filters from all locations except search
issue_body:
### Observed behavior

We've gotten feedback a couple times now that the type filters are confusing:

* https://github.com/learningequality/clearinghouse/issues/30
* recent feedback from VF that selecting content types seems broken

Part of the issue is that user feedback on type selection is poor, so users don't understand what the result of their action is.

Another issue is that type filters in the Recommended and Topic views are simply not that helpful: these views rarely show more than a dozen item. Filters are more helpful when a large (100's of items) list needs to be culled.

### Expected behavior

Filters should behave in a reasonable way, and should not be present when they're not useful.

### User-facing consequences

People are confused by our type filters and think they're broken.

issue_title: [Profiling] Humongous CPU with event_stream
issue_body:
I recently developed with the help of your client, which is quite awesome, a listener for marathon events using the event_stream() method.

The idea is simple, listen for the "deployment_step_success" event and execute some internal business code when this happens.

During the development/test phase I used a local marathon installation to provide the events to my code but when the code went live some problems started to happen.

The code seemed much slower than I expected and the CPU usage was ginormous.

After a quick profiling I discovered the problem was lying on the SSECLIENT lib that is used behind the scenes then I extracted the bit of code that actually listens for the events and performed another test.

```python
from marathon import MarathonClient

marathon_client = MarathonClient(marathon_address)

for _event in marathon_client.event_stream():
    print("Got event : {}".format(_event.event_type))
```

**OBS: The mesos/marathon cluster on production is around 60 apps with 200 tasks total**

Regression: Failure on options in layer.yaml
issue_body:
## Checklist

 - [X] Confirmed this is an issue with charm-tools, not charmstore-client
 - [X] Provide versions of tools used
 - [X] Described the feature or ways to replicate the issue

## What version am I running?

I ran the following command: `charm version` and got the following output:

_It's complicated._

Using the snap:
```
$ which charm
/snap/bin/charm
$ charm version
charm error
charm-tools 2.1.2
```
Using the deb from universe:
```
$ which charm
/usr/bin/charm
$ charm version
charm 2.1.1-0ubuntu1
charm-tools 2.1.2
```

The issue is also present in `master` at `36d72f31dc50ca7fb707697a1608f6c94d0c142e`.
I am using: xenial

## Issue/Feature

Trying to build a charm I get:
```
$ charm build
build: Composing into /home/bac/charms
build: Destination charm directory: /home/bac/charms/builds/bundleservice
build: Processing layer: layer:basic
build: Processing layer: layer:apt
build: Processing layer: layer:nagios
build: Processing layer: bundleservice
build: Processing interface: nrpe-external-master
charmtools.build.tactics: Options set for undefined layer: apt
```
and nothing is created in the `build` directory.


### I expect/expected the following

The charm command should not have complained and the charm should be built.

### What I got

See above.

The error is produced at: 

https://github.com/juju/charm-tools/blob/master/charmtools/build/tactics.py#L516

Debugging I see that `defined_layer_names` includes `layer:basic` and `layer:apt`.  The `option_layer_names` includes `apt` with no 'layer' prefix. This is the crux of the problem.

The layer.yaml file is:

```
includes: ['layer:basic', 'layer:apt', 'layer:nagios', 'interface:nrpe-external-master']
repo: https://github.com/juju/bundleservice-charm.git
options:
  apt:
    packages:
      - bundle service
```

This naming follows the example in 
https://jujucharms.com/docs/2.0/reference-layer-yaml#layer-options

If, however, we temporarily change the name in `options` to `layer:apt` then the charm builds ok.

Also note that if I install version 2.1.4 of charm-tools from pip then I can use `charm-build` and it works as expected.

The change to all availabilityZone for Volumes breaks rest versions 1 & 2 usage
https://github.com/profitbricks/profitbricks-sdk-python/commit/f3d7de308381981e71d100777edeb8ae6b65faea#diff-a6b8a93c04c0a636e1e6472d1b33762eR2263

availabilityZone is not required in v3, and therefor should not be defaulted to `AUTO`
```
availabilityZone	string	The storage availability zone assigned to the volume. Valid values: AUTO, ZONE_1, ZONE_2, or ZONE_3. This only applies to HDD volumes. Leave blank or set to AUTO when provisioning SSD volumes.	No
```
https://devops.profitbricks.com/api/cloud/v3/#create-volume

issue_title: Support for multiple GPUs
issue_body:
TensorFrames should be able to run tasks concurrently on separate GPUs, if they are available. This should be possible with the latest TensorFlow java API:
https://www.tensorflow.org/api_docs/java/reference/org/tensorflow/Session.html#Session(org.tensorflow.Graph, byte[])

Assuming that all the GPUs are available for running tasks, they can be locked onto specific sessions in python by using the following code:
```python
config = tf.ConfigProto()
config.gpu_options.visible_device_list = "0" # Or different values
session = tf.Session(config=config)
```

issue_title: High memory usage when configuration file contains ~150k routes

issue_body:
Docker-py don't seems compliant with the multi-dir wildcard syntax in .dockerignore.

The corresponding docker issue is https://github.com/docker/docker/issues/13113 and the PR is https://github.com/docker/docker/pull/17090.

It's painful especially for Python projects because we need several level of simple wildcards to ignore `.pyc` files (see this comment: https://github.com/docker/docker/issues/13113#issuecomment-124209405).

I've pushed a git repository to reproduce the issue: https://github.com/Lothiraldan/docker-py-wildcarddir-bug

Building it with the official docker client outputs:

```

```

It seems that the docker PR replaced the equivalent of `match_path` (https://github.com/docker/docker-py/blob/b3ea1fb9421ceb8d4c064a02d34594eaeb910781/docker/utils/utils.py#L228-L235) by a regex here https://github.com/docker/docker/pull/17090/files#diff-d293110531a98dc2d3a48d6f48226c51R122.

I don't know if there is a better option to support it in Python. I've also checked https://pypi.python.org/pypi/glob2/0.5 as it seems to support it but the double-wildcard support `**` is made recursively at the directory walking level and not the path matching level.

Python version: 3.5.2
OS: Mac OS X 10.11.6
Docker-py: master (https://github.com/docker/docker-py/commit/b3ea1fb9421ceb8d4c064a02d34594eaeb910781)

I'm planning to take some times trying to do a PR for the support.

issue_title: client.logs(stream=True) is broken when using docker 1.7
issue_body:
@shin- 

The streaming functionality of client.logs is broken when using docker 1.7.  I am working on a fix and have found the reason that it is now broken:
Line line 130 in the file daemon/logs.go
```
// write an empty chunk of data (this is to ensure that the
// HTTP Response is sent immediatly, even if the container has
// not yet produced any data)
outStream.Write(nil)
```

This messes up how `_multiplexed_response_stream_helper` works (will break if it sees a response like this).

I would really appreciate it if you could explain:

1. Why the test was removed
2. Steps I need to take to put the tests back and merge my fix. 


Alex

issue_title: Add niScope_wfmInfo support to NI-SCOPE
Several properties / functions use enums as constants
issue_body:
For example, property `powerline_freq` uses enum `PowerlineFrequency` which actually contains constants for 50.0 and 60.0. These aren't actually enumerated values and arguably make the API harder to use because there are more types to know about.

* These were inherited from CVI APIs.
* In many cases in LabVIEW we don't use enums for the equivalent properties.
* In C, we do often provide these values as constants, but not enums.

issue_title: Special error/warning for generator-methods on API overrides?
issue_body:
See also https://github.com/crossbario/txaio/issues/56

It is easy to forget to add ``@coroutine`` or ``@inlineCallbacks`` and accidentally get a generator-function instead of a Deferred/Future-returning function (i.e. if you put a ``yield`` in the method). Since there's no way that an actual generator-function will work correctly for ``onJoin`` and friends, it would be nice if Autobahn detected this and raised an error. I think this is a good idea.

There's a further argument (in the above-linked txaio ticket) that maybe this should apply to *any* and *all* functions passed to ``txaio.as_future`` (with some way to opt-out on a per-invocation basis). I'm not necessarily sold on this, but it's also very rare to *actually* want to pass a generator back from an async method -- however, this would have effects on downstream authors (e.g. those who *do* want to actually pass a generator back) as they'd have to a) know about ``txaio``; and b) use ``txaio``'s decorator to "opt out" of the error.

issue_title: Graceful Shutdown does not remove from Discovery URL
issue_body: The graceful shutdown script will not remove nodes from the Discovery URL.  The effect of this is that the new node that comes up with be a peer always.  So if one of the nodes that is contributing to the quorum goes down and gets replaced the quorum will stay -1.  If you attempt to replace all of your initial nodes or even most it is likely you would lose the cluster.

issue_title: Bug (contrib/azure) - unable to add new discovery URL to azure-user-data
issue_body: The command `./create-azure-user-data $(curl -s https://discovery.etcd.io/new)` did not correctly parse the `user-data-template` file to replace value of DISCOVERY_URL in the `azure-user-data` file. The end result was  `discovery: null`. This was also mentioned in #4593

issue_title: deis limits:set not persistent after upgrade
issue_body:
I upgraded from `1.2.1` -> `1.4.1` and everything seems to be working fine except all of the custom limits I had set on a per-app basis had disappeared and now are 'unset' to `Unlimited`..

    [bcochran@schala a-demo|master *]$ deis releases
    === a-demo Releases
    v4      Sun Feb 15 10:49:51 2015 CST bcochran deployed b803d82

    v1      Sun Jan 18 13:14:15 2015 CST bcochran created initial release
    
    [bcochran@schala a-demo|master *]$ deis limits
    === a-demo Limits
    
    --- Memory
    Unlimited
    
    --- CPU
    Unlimited

Just yesterday I set these to 712M and 1024 respectively.  It seems those revisions get wiped clean upon upgrade and do not get reset properly.

issue_title: Proposal integrating kubernetes with deis
issue_body:
Once we start a CoreOS machine with flannel, an overlay network is created for containers. Each container gets a unique IP address.
Here are some changes and proposals :
1. The app container port shouldn't be bind-mounted
2. Publisher should be able to publish the container IP as well as the port
3. The only relevant JobStates are Up, Destroyed, Crashed, Error
4. Scale logic should move from models.py to each scheduler backend
5. Template the user-data to allow custom flannel interfaces and IPs, like "make discovery-url" does
6. Give deis-registry a unique hostname URL, or run it on every host
7. Create a Kubernetes service for each app and let the router read from etcd endpoint of the service. This should avoid writing a custom publisher for Kubernetes.

Hi,
I am using tsfresh in the field of Anomaly detection. First of all, I am only training on the positive values and testing it on a mixture of positive and negative values. pipe=Pipeline(

    ('scaler',StandardScaler())
])

My third question: I am handling different users and it takes a lot of time for tsfresh to calculate the relevant features( sometimes close to 30 mins). Is there any way such that for the first time it runs on a particular user and selects some features and we could save this preference. issue_title: NaN objects in object columns of pandas dataframes from read_csv()
issue_body:
When trying to use `executemanycolumns()` with a pandas dataframe like this:

```
df = pd.read_csv(localfilepath,compression=csvfmt.compression,header=csvfmt.headerline,sep=csvdialect.delimiter,quotechar=csvdialect.quotechar,quoting=csvdialect.quoting,encoding=csvfmt.encoding,infer_datetime_format=True)

#some datetime conversion here

TURBODBC_OPTIONS = turbodbc.make_options(read_buffer_size=turbodbc.Megabytes(100),
                                                         parameter_sets_to_buffer=20000)
connection = turbodbc.connect(dsn=dsn, turbodbc_options=TURBODBC_OPTIONS)
cursor_local = connection.cursor()

to_insert = [(c, v) for c, v in df.iteritems()]
columns = [v.values for _, v in to_insert]
query = "INSERT INTO {}.{} ({}) VALUES ({})".format(schema,table,','.join([c for c, _ in to_insert]),
                                                                 ','.join(['?' for _, _ in to_insert]))
cursor.executemanycolumns(query,columns)
```

I get `RuntimeError: Unable to cast Python instance to C++ type (complie in debug mode for details)`.

The issue turned out to be that `read_csv()`represents missing strings as `NaN`objects in `object`columns rather than `None`objects.

It would be great if turbodbc could deal with those directly.

issue_title: Calling Filter.remove(*values) with no values produces unhandled error
issue_body:
* Version: 0.1.0b27
* Python: 3.6.5
* OS: linux


### What was wrong?
I am using the snapshot functionality to catch and revert a TransactionFailed error when automining transactions. I often (70% of the time in my test suite) run into an issue when reverting to a snapshot where it catches the following unhandled exception in this function call:
https://github.com/ethereum/eth-tester/blob/c51beb505f55f3dfd687d8b092a2afbb28452a0f/eth_tester/utils/filters.py#L47-L60

```python
    self._t.revert_to_snapshot(self._snapshot_id)
eth_tester/main.py:543: in revert_to_snapshot
    self._revert_log_filter(log_filter)
eth_tester/main.py:590: in _revert_log_filter
    filter.remove(*values_to_remove)
eth_tester/utils/filters.py:62: in remove
    in self.get_all()

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

.0 = <tuple_iterator object at 0x7f2585dfdef0>

    value
    for value
    in self.get_all()
>   if value not in values_to_remove
            ]
E           TypeError: unhashable type: 'dict'

eth_tester/utils/filters.py:63: TypeError

```

When this error occurs, the following variables of interest are set:
```python
values = ()  # Empty tuple

# Tuple of 1 dict
self.get_all() = ({'type': 'mined', 'log_index': 0, 'transaction_index': 0, 'transaction_hash': b'_\x0f;\xd1n/@pq\xcaL\xea\xd1\x11\xe5\x86\x03\xc3\xe9.\xd4\x88\x8b9\xfaHD\xc3\xa3;V\x1d', 'block_hash': b'\xcf\x9b\xec\xcaD[\x7fm\xc0KJ\x1c{\x80.Z\t\xa8\xb7fV\xe5\xb9X\x05\xe0N\x85E\xb0\xc0.', 'block_number': 2, 'address': b'\xf2\xe2F\xbbv\xdf\x87l\xef\x8b8\xae\x84\x13\x0fOU\xde9[', 'data': b'', 'topics': (b'\x8b\xe0\x07\x9cS\x16Y\x14\x13D\xcd\x1f\xd0\xa4\xf2\x84\x19I\x7f\x97"\xa3\xda\xaf\xe3\xb4\x18okdW\xe0', b'\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00+Z\xd5\xc4y\\\x02e\x14\xf81|z!^!\x8d\xcc\xd6\xcf', b'\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00+Z\xd5\xc4y\\\x02e\x14\xf81|z!^!\x8d\xcc\xd6\xcf')},)

values_to_remove = set()  # Empty set

```

### How can it be fixed?
I haven't been able to replicate this issue as it is inconsistent for me during runtime in my testing tool, but I think handling when `values` is unset is fairly obvious, since this basic Python also raises the same error:
```python
[v for v in ({},) if v not in set()]

TypeError: unhashable type: 'dict'
```

I can submit a quick fix for this, but I need a little more instruction on how to handle this scenario

I am thinking of suggesting the following:
```python
    queued_values = self.get_changes()
    if values_to_remove:
        self.values = [
            value
            for value
            in self.get_all()
            if value not in values_to_remove
        ]
    else:
        self.values = self.get_all()
```

issue_title: conda broken when using older versions of argcomplete
issue_body:
Using conda with a version of argcomplete prior the the introduction of argcomplete.CompletionFinder (for example version 0.6.7) results in a unusable conda command.   The Traceback is as follows:

```
$ conda info
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/Users/jhelmus/anaconda/bin/conda", line 5, in <module>
    sys.exit(main())
  File "/Users/jhelmus/anaconda/lib/python2.7/site-packages/conda/cli/main.py", line 106, in main
    from conda.cli import conda_argparse
  File "/Users/jhelmus/anaconda/lib/python2.7/site-packages/conda/cli/conda_argparse.py", line 41, in <module>
    class CondaSubprocessCompletionFinder(argcomplete.CompletionFinder):
AttributeError: 'module' object has no attribute 'CompletionFinder' 
```

Since conda is broken one cannot use conda to update argcomplete.  A workaround is to use pip to upgrade argcomplete to a compatible version compatible and then use conda to reinstall argcomplete if desired.  

A check could be added to the try/except block in the conda_argparse.py module to detect this.

issue_title: Trash clean up still very slow for various operations
issue_body:
In either conda 4.2.13 or 4.3.1, various operations tend to hang for a long time after they've completed. This appears to still be because of the exponential back-off for retries in the trash clean up code, and every now and then during this hang (under 4.3.1) it will spit out a series of errors like this:

`WARNING conda.gateways.disk:exp_backoff_fn(45): Uncaught backoff with errno 41`

(41 is ENOTEMPTY of course.)

I wrote about this in my last couple of comments on #3664 and I'll just summarise that here:

1. I still find it necessary to hack MAX_TRIES (now in gateways.disk.__init__.py) to 1 to get all operations to perform reasonably.
2. I think in some cases the retries will never succeed.
3. It's not clear to me why retries are desirable at all.

Let me know if you need any further info.

issue_title: Proposal: Use jinja2 templates to configure conda features automatically
issue_body:
## Summary

Conda `features` are a great tool to distinguish variants of the same package, but manual configuration of features does not scale when this capability gets used for a wide variety of properties. This issue proposes to apply the existing jinja2 templating mechanism to configure features automatically at package build time. Recipes equipped with suitable jinja2 code can be build with a specific set of properties in three simple steps:
- Create an environment where the desired features are tracked.
- Activate that environment.
- Run `conda build my-package`. It applies jinja2 preprocessing to `my-package/meta.yaml`, re-creates the current setup in the temporary `_build` environment and then compiles my-package with the desired features.

The proposed solution does not require changes to conda itself, just enhanced recipes. A successful preliminary implementation (which differs from the present proposal in some details) can be found in the recipe collection https://github.com/ilastik/ilastik-build-conda/tree/toolset-config.
## Rationale

One and the same version of a package can exist in several incompatible variants. For example, we may compile boost with different compilers (say, vc10 and vc14) and link the boost::python library against different Python versions (say, 2.7 and 3.5) to end up with four incompatible builds of the same boost version. Similarly, we may build numpy with various choices of the BLAS library such as Intel MKL or OpenBLAS. Other packages that also require BLAS should then choose the same variant as numpy did to ensure compatibility.

Conda uses clever conventions to avoid coexistence of incompatible variants whenever possible:
- There is a one-to-one correspondence between Python versions and compiler versions.
- Packages are updated in lock-step so that the set of newest versions (at any given time) is compatible.

When this is not possible, conda encodes compatibility information into the build string. For example, `_np110py27` means that a package requires Python 2.7 and numpy 1.10. This approach works reasonably, but is not always reliable because conda's version resolution algorithm treats build strings as secondary entities: (i) build strings are only looked at when version and build numbers are identical; (ii) build string comparison only supports exact matching, no relational comparisons or wild cards as in version numbers.

Moreover, the above conventions may be too restrictive in certain situations: Some users may be forced by external circumstances to use a different compiler than conda's default, or may be restricted to a particular version of a package so that packages cannot be updated in lockstep. Therefore, conda needs a more powerful mechanism to specify requirements for compatible variants.

Fortunately, such a mechanism already exists: conda's 'features' capability. For example, to ensure that a package is only used in combination with packages that were built by the same compiler, a recipe would specify:

```
build:
  features:
    - vc10
```

if the package requires Visual Studio 2010. As a consequence, this particular package would only be considered in conda environments that are based on this compiler. A conda environment acquires this feature-enforcing behavior by installing a package which tracks the feature, i.e. whose recipe contains:

```
build:
  track_features:
    - vc10
```

To make full use of 'features', three questions need to be answered:
1. Which packages need to enforce (i.e. track) features in order to ensure that only compatible dependents are installed, and what conventions/standards shall be observed to specify those features?
2. How can we insert the appropriate 'features' and 'track_features' specifications into the recipes automatically, especially when requirements are transitive?
3. How can we guarantee that 'conda install' always respects the feature specifications?
## 1) Which features?

As an introductory example, consider the problem of incompatible compilers on Windows. Conda already uses the features 'vc10', 'vc14' etc. to distinguish compilers on windows. Since each Python version in conda is tied to a particular compiler version, the corresponding 'track_features' spec is found in the Python recipe. This leads to the slight oddity that pure C++ packages must still list Python as a build requirement in order to activate the appropriate compiler (Insteadt, it might be better to create a dedicated 'visual-studio' package which installs the appropriate runtime libaries and activates the appropriate 'track_features'.) More importantly, it prevents users from compiling Python with a different compiler. For example, it is impossible to build extension modules that require C++11 for Python 2.7 on Windows because Visual Studio 2008 (conda's default for Python 2.7) only supports C++98. Conversely, one cannot use Python 3.5 when one is forced (by whatever reason) to an older compiler than conda's default.

At present, this problem is most pronounced on Windows, because Visual Studio versions produce mutually incompatible code. In contrast, the gcc 4.x compiler series on Linux generally generate compatible binaries. However, incompatibilities may arise with the more widespread use of gcc 5.x and clang, and by incompatible versions of the C++ standard library (libstdc++ vs. libc++ on Mac is a case in point). Moreover, compilers are not the only source of incompatibility, as the above example of numpy with MKL vs. numpy with OpenBLAS demonstrates. I will look into this example in the next section. In conclusion:
1. Packages where the same version number exists in multiple, binary incompatible variants must define (i.e. `track_features`) a feature.
2. Dependents of such packages must be built in the same variants, and those variants are distinguished by the appropriate `features` specification.
3. Feature names should follow the established conventions of 'vc10' and 'vc14' or 'np100' and 'np110'.
4. The set of properties supported by features must be clearly documented in the conda manuals. There should be an well-documented open process to discuss and accept proposed additions to the set.
## 2) How to add feature specs to recipes automatically?

At present, conda features are specified manually in a package recipe's `meta.yaml` file, e.g.

```
build:
  features:
    - vc9   # [win and py27]
    - vc10  # [win and py34]
    - vc14  # [win and py35]
```

to select the appropriate compiler runtime according to the Python version. However, this approach does not scale to more widespread application of the 'features' mechanism, and is not flexible enough to allow Python to be compiled with a non-standard compiler.

However, the actual difficulty is more fundamental: `meta.yaml` are static text files by nature, whereas the information about features is only available at build time (because it depends on the build environment), and may differ between different builds of the same recipe. Conda currently solves this by means of conditionals (the `[win and py27]` above), but this technique is severely limited by the restricted set of supported conditions.

But once again, conda already offers a solution: the possibility to customize `meta.yaml` files at build time by means of jinja2 preprocessing. A simple `meta.yaml` that configures the compiler feature at build time looks like this:

```
# import the feature spec of the active compiler
{% import '$CONDA_ACTIVE_ENV/conda-meta/features/compiler.yaml' as compiler %}

package:
  name: zlib
  version: "1.2.8"

build:
  features:
    - {{compiler.feature}}  # add feature for the current build
                            # (expands into 'vc10', 'vc14' etc. as appropriate)

requirements:
  build:
    - {{compiler.build_requirement}}  # list the correct compiler as a requirement

  run:
    - {{compiler.run_requirement}}    # list the compiler runtime as a requirement

source:
  ... # as usual
about:
  ... # as usual
```

Specifically, for a vc14 (Visual Studio 2015) build, the variables resolve into

```
{{compiler.feature}}            => vc14
{{compiler.build_requirement}}  => visual-studio-build  14.0
{{compiler.run_requirement}}    => visual-studio        14.0
```

The file `conda-meta/features/compiler.yaml` is installed by the command `conda install visual-studio-build=14.0` which prepares the current environment for conda builds with this compiler. Ideally, this command would also install the compiler into the current environment, but since this is not permitted by Microsoft's license and would be too time consuming anyway, it just checks that the correct compiler is in the PATH, and terminates otherwise (e.g. if it finds Visual Studio 10.0 instead). The package `visual-studio` just installs the compiler runtime DLLs. Moreover, both packages contain a `track-features:` specification with the appropriate feature tag.

The technique can be easily extended to other sources of variation. For example, anaconda currently enforces a one-to-one relationship between compilers and Python versions (e.g. Python 2.7 is tied to Visual Studio 2008 on Windows). Thus, the compiler feature implies the Python version. If anaconda drops this restriction, an additional feature will be needed to distinguish between Python versions, e.g. `py27` and `py35`. Another interesting case is the BLAS library to be used by numpy, where users may want to choose between Atlas, Intel MKL, OpenBLAS and possibly others. Once numpy is compiled against a specific BLAS library, all other packages that need BLAS should use the same one. This is again best handled by a feature that takes values like `blas_atlas3_10`, `blas_mkl11_3`, `blas_open0_2`.

Using features for the compiler, Python version and BLAS variant, the numpy recipe's `meta.yaml` may look like

```
{% import '$CONDA_ACTIVE_ENV/conda-meta/features/compiler.yaml' as compiler %}
{% import '$CONDA_ACTIVE_ENV/conda-meta/features/python.yaml'   as python %}
{% import '$CONDA_ACTIVE_ENV/conda-meta/features/blas.yaml'     as blas %}

package:
  name: numpy
  version: "1.10.1"

build:
  features:
    - {{compiler.feature}}
    - {{python.feature}}
    - {{blas.feature}}

requirements:
  build:
    - {{compiler.build_requirement}}
    - {{python.build_requirement}}
    - {{blas.build_requirement}}
    - fftw
    - cython
    - setuptools

  run:
    - {{compiler.run_requirement}}
    - {{python.run_requirement}}
    - {{blas.run_requirement}}
    - fftw

source:
  ... # as usual
about:
  ... # as usual
```

In summary, to build a package variant aginst a specific collection of dependencies, you
- Create an environment that contains the desired dependencies. Specifically, the required jinja2 template files must be installed under `conda-meta/features/`.
- Activate that environment.
- Run `conda build pkg-name`. It applies jinja2 preprocessing to `meta.yaml`, re-creates the current setup in the temporary `_build` environment and then compiles the new package with the desired features.

No changes to conda itself are needed to implement the approach outlined above - everything can be achieved by providing suitable recipes. Specifically, packages that track features must specify the appropriate `track_features` tag and install a jinja2 template under `conda-meta/features/`. Packages that want to use a feature must read the jinja2 template from `conda-meta/features/` and apply the appropriate configurations in the `features` and `requirements` sections of their `meta.yaml` file.
## 3) How to enforce features in 'conda install'?

In theory, conda's version resolution algorithm resolves feature requirements automatically, provided the settings have been correctly configuered in the package recipes. Unfortunately, the present version resolution has a bug that prevents this from working in all situations, see issue https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/1589 and references therein for details. It appears to be difficult to fix this bug without making version resolution very slow.

A possible work-around is to add feature tags to the version number. For example, zlib compiled with Visual Studio 2015 would become `zlib-1.2.8.vc14`. This is similar to conda's current convention to add feature tags to the build string, but is much more powerful: it makes all the sophistication of conda's version comparison available to feature matching as well. It is easy to use this technique to make sure that all packages in a given conda environment are built with the same compiler. Foe example, when we build the recipe for libtiff with Visual Studio 2015, we can specify its dependency on zlib as:

```
requirements:
  run:
    - zlib   *.vc14*
```

Incompatible zlib variants lack the `.vc14` tag in their version number and will thus be excluded from version resolution. Hard-wiring a specific tag value can again be avoided by means of jinja2 variables:

```
{% import '$CONDA_ACTIVE_ENV/conda-meta/features/compiler.yaml' as compiler %}

requirements:
  run:
    - zlib   *{{compiler.version_tag}}*
```

Version tags would also simplify the process of setting up a conda environment with specific properties. For example, to create an environment containing scipy and numpy build against the OpenBLAS library, one could simply write

```
conda create -n scipy-openblas   scipy=0.16.1.blas_open0_2
```

This command would recursively install openblas and numpy with the desired feature.

A potential problem with version tags is that a version number should probably only contain at most two or three of them to avoid clutter. Thus, issue https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/1589 still needs to be solved. Nonetheless, version tags may remain a useful technique to simplify conda installation commands.

Of course, many details need to be discussed and improved before this proposal can be made an official part of the conda infrastructure. However, a working preliminary implementation (which uses jinja2 templates for just the compiler configuration and differs in various details from the present text) can be found in the recipe collection https://github.com/ilastik/ilastik-build-conda/tree/toolset-config. It provides around 130 recipes to compile a Python 2.7 ecosystem with Visual Studio 2012 and has worked very well for creating the ilastik interactive learning and segmentation toolkit on Windows (see http://ilastik.org).

Please comment!

issue_title: Consider shell-agnostic activate.d/deactivate.d mechanism
issue_body:
Currently `activate.d` and `deactivate.d` directories contain files meant to be sourced by the currently-active shell.  E.g. `.sh` files for bash shells; `.bat` files for cmd.exe shells.  In those two files alone there's potential need for duplicated logic.  It's also a common problem that `.sh` files are often naively written for bash, but should in fact be written for posix shell compliance.  These shell-specific files also completely neglect powershell, xonsh, tcsh, fish, and any other future shells we might easily support for conda with a simple shell wrapper addition.

We therefore need a shell-agnostic method for specifying changes to process environment variables during environment activation and deactivation.

Here's how things look using conda 4.1.3 for a `cd` into a git folder:

┌─( ~ )───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────( @MacL2938 )─┐

(stable) └─❱❱❱                                                            +4603 17:02 ❰─┘

Here's how it looks with conda 4.1.4:
```zsh

prepending /Users/klay6683/miniconda3/envs/stable/bin to PATH
git-info:165: bad math expression: operator expected at `0'

During testing, I made sure that I complete restart my terminal. I did that because the above git-info error symptom remains even after a `source deactivate`!

The `zsh` community had a similar problem 2 years ago, see sorin-ionescu/prezto#566.

issue_title: newer version of pexpect needed for jupyter
issue_body:
After running `conda install jupyter` in an environment with Python 2 and starting `ipython` caused  a`No module named exceptions` exception.  This problem was already reported at [ImportError: No module named exceptions · Issue #9065 · ipython/ipython](https://github.com/ipython/ipython/issues/9065#issuecomment-176909684).  I copy what I [wrote on that thread](https://github.com/ipython/ipython/issues/9065#issuecomment-176909684) as a way of reporting the problem to the `conda` developers:

I just ran into this problem while installing `jupyter` in a new conda env on Mac OS X 10.9.5.  

After doing:
```
conda create -n ipyw-test python=2 pip
. activate ipyw-test
conda install jupyter
```
I got an error running `ipython` because of the missing `exceptions` package.  I used the [suggested fix](https://github.com/ipython/ipython/issues/9065#issuecomment-162163512):

```
conda remove pexpect
conda clean --tarballs --packages
conda install pexpect
```
but then had to add

```
pip install -U pexpect
```

because conda didn't have the correct version of `pexpect`.

issue_title: pyproj package is built incorrectly.
issue_body:
The pyproj package is built incorrectly and as a result the proj.4 library isn't being linked properly.  This has incorrectly been identified as an issue on the proj.4 issue tracker here:

https://github.com/jswhit/pyproj/issues/17

but the anaconda.org package available here:

https://anaconda.org/ioos/pyproj

installs and works fine.

issue_title: Package cache locations during installs
issue_body: Given the new multi-user cache support in conda, it would be really useful to see which caches conda is interacting with during a fetch/link.  Or another behavior is to only show the cache location for a package if its not from the main/global cache.

issue_title: change of package channel when downgrading another package

issue_title: Python `mkl` slower than `nomkl`
issue_body:
My machine is 32 cores.

I use the really simple benchmark:
```
python -m timeit -s 'import numpy as np; A = np.random.rand(5000, 5000); b = np.random.rand(5000);' 'A.dot(b)'
```
And the results are:

- numpy from Ubuntu
`100 loops, best of 3: 11.2 msec per loop`

- numpy with `mkl`: `conda create -n mkl python=2.7 numpy mkl`
`10 loops, best of 3: 24.1 msec per loop`

- numpy with `nomkl`: `conda create -n nomkl python=2.7 numpy nomkl`
`10 loops, best of 3: 11.8 msec per loop`

The issue seems coming from the multiprocessing/thread, the `mkl` option does not and `nomkl` does.
After I have read blog [there](https://www.continuum.io/blog/developer-blog/anaconda-25-release-now-mkl-optimizations), then I have played around `mkl-service` without success (issue [#2](https://github.com/ContinuumIO/mkl-service/issues/2))

It is the same, Python 3.5 or 2.7

Note:
it is not the case on my desktop machine (8 cores), `mkl` vs `nomkl`:
100 loops, best of 3: 10.6 msec per loop
100 loops, best of 3: 11.8 msec per loop

issue_title: Better performance by subprocess
issue_body:
Hi,

I have been using pysam library to process, filter and merge BAM files, but I have been facing some performance issues. Comparing to a script using python subprocess module running `samtools` on shell, I get about twice faster results. Here's a single example trial:

```
~/D/w/tmp ❯❯❯ time python3 subprocess_reader.py
87329 reads were removed.
python3 subprocess_reader.py 6,56s user 0,13s system 58% cpu 11,410 total
~/D/w/tmp ❯❯❯ time python3 pysam_filter.py 
87329 reads were removed.
python3 pysam_filter.py 13,82s user 0,14s system 97% cpu 14,324 total
```

I understand the library must have some overhead to organize the reads information, but I don't believe it explains the difference of almost 2x. Any advice? Am I doing anything stupid maybe?

Follows the code for both alternatives:

_subprocess_

```
with open('temp_.bam', 'wb') as outstream:
    samview = subprocess.Popen(['samtools', 'view', '-h', sam_path],
                               stdout=subprocess.PIPE,
                               stderr=subprocess.DEVNULL)
    bamview = subprocess.Popen(['samtools', 'view', '-bS', '-'],
                               stdin=subprocess.PIPE,
                               stdout=outstream,
                               stderr=subprocess.DEVNULL)
    bamstream = bamview.stdin
    n = 0
    for line in samview.stdout:
        dline = line.decode('ascii')
        if not dline.startswith('@'):
            sequence = dline.split()[9]
            if get_edge(sequence) in to_filter:
                n += 1
                continue
        bamstream.write(line)
    print('%d reads were removed.' % n)
```

_pysam_

```
with AlignmentFile(sam_path, 'rb') as samfile, AlignmentFile(
        'temp.bam', 'wb', template=samfile) as filtered:
    n = 0
    for entry in samfile.fetch(until_eof=True):
        if get_edge(entry.query_sequence) not in to_filter:
            filtered.write(entry)
        else:
            n += 1
    print('%d reads were removed.' % n)
```

issue_title: Syntax highlighting: free format sets all lines starting with * as comment lines
issue_body:
Example:

~~~cobol
        compute a = a
                  * b
                  / 20
~~~

Free format has only inline comments, these start with `*>`.
There are other formats like the ACU terminal format (where comments start in col 1 with an asterisk, debugging lines on col 1 with `D`, ...) but as GnuCOBOL doesn't support it (yet) we may don't need to support this in OCIDE.
Or add a checkbox "asterisk in column 1 as inline comment" (MF has a similar setting which GnuCOBOL supports) in Preferences... In any case this would only be about col 1.

issue_title: Adding event support
issue_body:
Hi there!

I am working on a project for https://github.com/home-assistant/home-assistant to add support for receiving events from an ISY. Right now we use `PyISY` for all node updates, and it works very well for seeing and controlling the `status` of lights. However, it cannot be aware of when, for example, a human presses OFF on a lightswitch that was already off (or more importantly, when the user does something unusual like double-tapping a switch.) The events DO pass through the ISY and in to PyISY, but PyISY doesn't process the event because it only cares about status updates currently.

So, what I would like to see is the ability for the consumer of this app to be able to subscribe to "Control" events (as ISY calls them) in addition to the "Status" events that are already supported.

I would be up for adding this myself, but I am not actually sure what direction to go in this implementation!

1. `az resource show --id <id>`  should just work w/o any other parameters. This is the most important scenario.

issue_title: [App Service] Deployment slot list doesn't provide the actual "raw" slot name
issue_body:
If you run `az appservice web deployment slot swap -h`, you notice that you need to specify the name of the source deployment slot. However, if you run `az appservice web deployment list -ojsonc` in order to determine the value you can specify to the `swap` command, nowhere in that response do you actually get the "raw" slot name in the expected format. It responds with the following three properties:

1. `id` - The resource ID of the slot, in the format of `/subscriptions/<subscriptionId>/resourceGroups/<groupName>/providers/Microsoft.Web/sites/<appName>/slots/<slotName>`

2. `name` - A string in the format of `<appName>/<slotName>`.

3. `siteName` - A string in the format of `<appName>(<slotName>)`

If you tried to specify the exact value of any of those three properties to the `swap` command, it will fail. They all include the `<slotName>`, however, you'd need to know to parse that substring out and use it when trying to swap. This seems needlessly complex, as opposed to the list simply displaying the slot name that actually matters, and is what the user would have specified when creating it in the first place.

The portal displays the list of slots with their name and their state (e.g. `Running`). It would be great if the CLI provided the same experience, along the lines of the following example:

```
Name         State
-------------------
Staging      Running
```

issue_title: aiohttp.client does not support HTTP redirect 308 (RFC 7538)
issue_body:
Currently, `aiohttp.client` does not support recent HTTP redirect code 308 Permanent Redirect, defined in [RFC 7538](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7538). It is simply a permanent version of already handled 307 redirect, or a legacy-free version of 301 redirect.

I would also argue that `normalize_path_middleware` default redirect class should be changed to `HTTPPermanentRedirect`, as it is currently very surprising that a user agent will likely retry a POST request as a GET one after receiving a 301 redirect from path normalization, but that is probably a breaking API change.
Still, supporting 308 in `aiohttp.client` would allow `normalize_path_middleware` to be used with POST requests in asyncio <-> asyncio communication by specifying redirect 308 manually.

issue_title: canot set authorization header
issue_body:
## Long story short

<!-- Please describe your problem and why the fix is important. -->
Cannot set the Authorization header with this code.

## Steps to reproduce

```py
import asyncio
 
import aioec
 
loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
client = aioec.Client(token='BNI=;6pXUYxftBItgvTAtN6X7bDGDluCnEwOnavKG9i3A4MY=', loop=loop)
 
print(loop.run_until_complete(client.login()))
 ```
That is a test token, and safe to reproduce. It has no access other than authorization.

## Expected behaviour

`1234` is outputted.

## Actual behaviour

`aioec.errors.LoginFailure: Invalid or incorrect token has been passed.` is outputted

## Reason

It seems that the `Authorization` header is not being set. printing `response.request_info.headers['Authorization']` inside `aioec/http.HttpClient.request` raises a `KeyError: "Key not found: 'Authorization'"`, even though the headers are clearly set in [aioec/http.py at 5ae0994 L47-L151](https://github.com/EmojiConnoisseur/aioec/blob/5ae09946c0c83ebf311f825b8358b9082d6e3bde/aioec/http.py#L47-L51) and I can confirm that self._session._default_headers['Authorization'] is set.

## Workaround

**Downgrade to aiohttp>=2.0.0,<2.3.0 and yarl<1.2.**
This is why I believe the issue to be aiohttp based and not in my code, unless I'm using aiohttp incorrectly.

## Your environment

<!-- Describe the environment you have that lead to your issue.
     This includes aiohttp version, OS, proxy server and other bits that
     are related to your case. 
     
     IMPORTANT: aiohttp is both server framework and client library.
     For getting rid of confusing please put 'server', 'client' or 'both'
     word here.
     -->
aiohttp version: 3.3.2
OS: Ubuntu 18.04 LTS
Python: 3.6.5

issue_title: Conflicts with pytest-asyncio (sample with redis)
issue_body:
Having following test file, things behave differently with different pytest plugins installed:

```python
import aioredis
import pytest
from aiohttp import web


def init():
    """Create web.Application instance
    """
    app = web.Application()
    app.router.add_get('/', hello)
    app["redis_url"] = "redis://localhost:6379"
    app.on_startup.append(create_redis_pool)
    app.on_shutdown.insert(0, close_redis_pool)
    return app


async def hello(request):
    """url Handler for hello"""
    return web.Response(text='Hello, world')


async def create_redis_pool(app):
    """Create Redis connection pool"""
    redis_url = app["redis_url"]
    pool = await aioredis.create_redis_pool(redis_url, loop=app.loop)
    app["redis_pool"] = pool


async def close_redis_pool(app):
    """Close Redis connection pool"""
    pool = app["redis_pool"]
    pool.close()
    await pool.wait_closed()


# @pytest.fixture
# def loop(event_loop):
#     """Ensure usable event loop for everyone.
#
#     If you comment this fixture out, default pytest-aiohttp one is used
#     and things start failing (when redis pool is in place).
#     """
#     return event_loop


@pytest.fixture
def app(loop):
    """Application instance.
    """
    return init()


@pytest.fixture
async def cli(test_client, app, loop):
    """HTTP client for our application.
    """
    return await test_client(app)


async def test_hello(cli, loop):
    resp = await cli.get("/")
    assert resp.status == 200
    text = await resp.text()
    assert "Hello, world" in text
```

# only `pytest-aiohttp` installed: all is fine

When only `pytest-aiohttp` is installed, the test runs well as it is.

```
============================================================================================= test session starts =============================================================================================
platform linux -- Python 3.6.3, pytest-3.4.0, py-1.5.2, pluggy-0.6.0
rootdir: /home/javl/devel/tis/dpo-tis-pns, inifile:
collected 1 item

tests/http/test_redisloop.py .                                                                                                                                                                          [100%]

========================================================================================== 1 passed in 0.06 seconds ===========================================================================================

[Process exited 0]
```

# with `pytest-asyncio`, it fails
When also `pytest-asyncio` is installed, the test fails with:

```
self = <ConnectionsPool [db:0, size:[1:10], free:0]>

    async def wait_closed(self):
        """Wait until pool gets closed."""
        await self._close_state.wait()
        assert self._close_waiter is not None
>       await asyncio.shield(self._close_waiter, loop=self._loop)
E       RuntimeError: Task <Task pending coro=<pytest_fixture_setup.<locals>.wrapper.<locals>.setup() running at /home/javl/devel/tis/dpo-tis-pns/.tox/py36/lib/python3.6/site-packages/pytest_asyncio/plugin.p
y:117> cb=[_run_until_complete_cb() at /home/javl/.pyenv/versions/3.6.3/lib/python3.6/asyncio/base_events.py:176]> got Future <Future pending> attached to a different loop

.tox/py36/lib/python3.6/site-packages/aioredis/pool.py:177: RuntimeError
>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>> entering PDB >>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>
[33] > /home/javl/devel/tis/dpo-tis-pns/.tox/py36/lib/python3.6/site-packages/aioredis/pool.py(177)wait_closed()

issue_title: Upload/Download Speed
issue_body:
**Looking into how to speed up upload/download**

For this first pass I am considering the scenario of uploading one large file rather than an upload with many little files. In my testing I looked at uploading [jksrc.zip](http://hgdownload.soe.ucsc.edu/admin/jksrc.zip)  a 85 MB file.

**Profiling current upload**
```
python -m cProfile -o jpb.prof ddsc/__main__.py upload -p 'ok' ../../../Downloads/jksrc.zip
python -m pstats jpb.prof
jpb.prof% sort tottime
jpb.prof% stats 10
...
271534 function calls (269800 primitive calls) in 46.449 seconds
...
   ncalls  tottime  percall  cumtime  percall filename:lineno(function)
       39   32.815    0.841   32.815    0.841 {method 'write' of '_ssl._SSLSocket' objects}
       41    9.206    0.225    9.206    0.225 {method 'read' of '_ssl._SSLSocket' objects}

time python -m ddsc upload -p 'timetest' ../../../Downloads/jksrc.zip
...
real	0m54.653s
```
**Notes**
This profiling was done on my macbook pro (16 GB Core i7 4 cores).
So 46.449 is the total time we spent doing work.
**tottime** is the time spent inside the method excluding methods/functions it calls.
**cumtime** is the time spent inside the method including methods/functions it calls.
So we spent the majority of the time outside of my code waiting for ssl socket writes.

**grequests**
This is a nodejs/libuv/libev style solution where you still have one process with multiple greenlets(green threads). This is an _almost_ drop in replacement for requests.
I tried using grequests to batch the uploads to swift data store for the one file and it made little difference.  This doesn't actually make more processes it just lets the requests go async with callbacks. Since we are stuck in _ssl._SSLSocket I think this result makes sense.

**multiprocessing.Pool**
This is a traditional fork style solution where you fork multiple processes and run work in them.
I created a Pool with the default settings(one for each cpu) and changed the uploads to swift data store for one file to use the pool. It then waits for all the pool to finish before sending DDS the create file command as I believe DDS checks with swift as part of that step.
```
time python -m ddsc upload -p 'timetest2' ../../../Downloads/jksrc.zip
...
real	0m14.090s
```
I've omitted the cProfile results since it doesn't handle multiple processes (the main process was still primarily ```read of _ssl._SSLSocket```).

I think we need to require the user to specify number of cpu since they will be likely using this on a shared server.

I need to consider(time test) other upload scenarios with multiple files.

issue_title: asyncio.async() is deprecated
issue_body:
The new supported function is `asyncio.ensure_future`. The deprecation causes warnings to appear in our tests.

Unfortunately, `ensure_future` doesn't appear to be supported in the versions of the asyncio library that are available for Python 3.3. We could introduce a hack to choose a different function depending on what Python we're in. Or we could consider dropping support for 3.3. I don't think it's got very widespread usage, and supporting it complicates our setup.py.

I'm leaning towards the hack, since it should be pretty contained. Just wanted to think about it.

issue_title: Support `peru reup --dry-run`
Packages must have at least one pure python module
issue_body:
Due to how the build extension modules are shipped as "package data", they are not shipped because:

  https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/blob/29c8385814335e91538d76b68578b6c0ab483c1a/setuptools/command/sdist.py#L147

One approach is to find some way to override distutils.dist.Distribution.has_pure_modules().

Another approach would check that there is at least one pure python module, and warn the user that this bug exists.

Another approach is to stop marking the generated C-extension as "package data", list them as the output of a custom *build_ext* command, and make sure they get added to the package manifest.

issue_title: Mention pycrypto installation problem in README
issue_body:
_From @hannes-ucsc on April 22, 2015 5:42_

User ran `sudo pip install git+https://github.com/BD2KGenomics/cgcloud-core.git`

Failed with 

```
    src/MD2.c:31:20: fatal error: Python.h: No such file or directory
     #include "Python.h"
                        ^
```

User installed python-dev: `sudo apt-get -y install python-dev`

User reran above `pip install` command:

```
ubuntu@ip-172-31-21-203:~$ sudo pip install --process-dependency-links git+https://github.com/BD2KGenomics/cgcloud-core.git
Downloading/unpacking git+https://github.com/BD2KGenomics/cgcloud-core.git

Requirement already satisfied (use --upgrade to upgrade): bd2k-python-lib==1.5 in /usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages (from cgcloud-core==1.0.dev1)

Requirement already satisfied (use --upgrade to upgrade): pkginfo>=1.1 in /usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages (from cgcloud-core==1.0.dev1)

Requirement already satisfied (use --upgrade to upgrade): Fabric>=1.7.0 in /usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages (from cgcloud-core==1.0.dev1)

Cleaning up...

So the installation of pycrypto was skipped even though it never completed successfully. User then ran cgcloud and hit this:

```

Traceback (most recent call last):

    needed = self.resolve(parse_requirements(requirements))

    raise DistributionNotFound(req, requirers)

User installed pycrypto explicitly: `sudo pip install pycrypto`. This went through because now `python-dev` was installed. That fixed the problem.

Test assumes that you already have pytest-selenium installed into your virtualenv. 

Create a simple test called test_debug_pytest_html.py

```python
import pytest


class TestDebugPytestHtml(object):

Now run this test from the command `py.test --driver Firefox --html test_debug_pytest_html.py`

issue_title: Profile does not work with geckodriver+remote webdriver
issue_body:
geckodriver changed the desired capability from `firefox_profile` (https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest-splinter/blob/8e4cefdf98caff9004b0a574b70866fe2bbbcb1f/pytest_splinter/plugin.py#L307).

Instead, per https://github.com/mozilla/geckodriver#firefox-capabilities, `moz:firefoxOptions`/`profile` should be used.

I've verified that specfying `moz:firefoxOptions`/`profile` works as intended.

Forking "partners" dashboard app without replacing "partner" base app
issue_body:
It seems replacing/forking the **oscar.apps.dashboard.partners** dashboard app without also replacing its "base" app (**oscar.apps.partner**) is not possible at the moment, due to an issue with custom permission creation (_dashboard_access_).

A simple way to reproduce the bug is the following:
- Fork Oscar's default "partners" dashboard app.
- Replace it in INSTALLED_APPS: e.g. "my.apps.dashboard.partners"
- Sync the DB and launch the server.
- Go to _Fullfilment > Partners > partner_name > Link existing user_
- Try to link a user.

An error regarding the absence of "**partner.dashboard_access**" permission will be raised.

BUT, if you replace ALSO the base "partner" app (e.g. "my.apps.partner"), simply including the standard Oscar's models ("from oscar.apps.partner.models import *") the problem goes away.

issue_title: Question: How are you getting detailed error messages?
issue_body:
**Question**:

How is the python client getting such detailed error messages to deliver to the user?

Is it the error that's coming back in the http response, or is it extrapolated from a code number?

I just figured it would be easier to ask some people that know and can answer quickly rather than grep around the code for hours myself.

**Background**:

I had a misconfiguration with my DNS server which was prevented validation.

I first tried using Caddy (an LE client written in go, uses lego) which gave me this:

```
2015/12/06 03:51:44 [blog.coolaj86.com] failed to get certificate: The server could not validate our request.
```

I thought that perhaps the Caddy implementation was simply incomplete, so then I tried letsencrypt's own python client and got this:

```
Failed authorization procedure. blog.coolaj86.com (tls-sni-01): urn:acme:error:unknownHost :: The server could not resolve a domain name :: No IPv4 addresses found for blog.coolaj86.com
```

Over at Caddy Matt I filed an issue "ambiguous error message" (https://github.com/mholt/caddy/issues/393) and he says that all they're seeing is an `invalid` in the response from boulder, so they don't want what error message to give back.

Also following up with lego at https://github.com/xenolf/lego/issues/49

issue_title: Calling sudo from inside a script = bad
issue_body:
So typically people should be aware root actions are about to be performed when they type in a command.

$ letsencrypt --help would not instictually be thought of as a command needing root access.  It's fine that it does but not ideal.  What is troubling is the use of sudo inside the script.

This means that if they had just run a few commands as root (someone working on their https would be in server configuration mode) and then decided to read the most available cli documentation on your command.  BOOM.  Let's install all the things.

A better solution would be to reject running with and give a prompt stating that root access is need, then allow the user to explicitly grant it for your program.

The golden rule should be never include sudo in anything.  I can't think of any tool on debian that does.  I know on arch systemctl prompts for raised privilage but it doesn't use sudo because each issue requires granted privaliges.  Let the user manage root access.  Don't seripticiously gain root access through the back door without permission.

I'm sure you figured they would be prompted so it's all the same but sudo does have a grace period.  It is equal level of convinience for the user to sign for it on the outside of the program and is just polite.

Also considering this is a security project it gives people higher confidence that you know what you're doing.

Sorry to leave a potencially rude comment on your issues page.  I hope you don't read it that way.  Oviously in scale of rudeness I really don't have much to worry about as gaining root access without permission on someobodies server.  You can't go above that really.

Anyways hopefully this was just a mistake ;)

I'll see if I can make the edits for you guys.

issue_title: Confluent-kafka-python, Tornado and GIL
issue_body:
Let's talk about usage of **confluent-kafka-python** Producer together with Tornado web server.

We know that Tornado is non blocking-server and has event loop for processing socket events.
We also know that librdkafka has several threads and its own event loop. And confluent-kafka-python is build on top of librdkafka.

When we call Producer.produce() from something inside Tornado it executes in main thread. Right? Then librdkafka pass data (with callback, probably) to another thread (event loop thread). And when message succefully recieved by Kafka, callback executed.

What happens when librdkafka call callback function from its event loop? Does it lock Python main thread with GIL?

    """Make a black->red hex colour"""

    assert value <= mx
    percent = (1/(mx-mn)) * (value-mn)
    red_in_hex_range = int(255 * percent)
    # make a HTML color code e.g. #ff0000
    color = "#{:02x}0000".format(red_in_hex_range)

issue_title: External IP segment causes entire status line to fail if unable to connect
issue_body:
If Internet connection is down, or powerline is unable to resolve given URL, or any other network-based exception occurs during the call of `_external_ip(query_url)` in `segments/common/net.py`, that function raises an unhandled exception which causes entire powerline to display that exception.

From the user perspective, displaying a concise error message instead, something like `No connection` or disabling the segment altogether would provide a smoother user experience. I'm submitting a pull request which implements this behavior.

issue_title: jobnum Segment Shows '0' Regardless of show_zero Setting When Using zpython
issue_body:
### Issue
If you are using `zpython` in `zsh`, the `jobnum` segment will show `0` when there are zero jobs regardless of the `show_zero` setting.

### Expected Behavior
If there are zero background jobs, and `show_zero` is false, instead of displaying `0`, `powerline` should hide the segment.

### Recreating
This issue can be recreated by including the `jobnum` segment in your shell config and using `zsh` with `zpython` as the `powerline` renderer. I believe that `jobnum` is included in the default shell config, and by default `show_zero` is false.

### Cause
`jobnum()` in `powerline/segments/shell.py` treats `segment_info['args'].jobnum` as type `int` (and that is correct when `zpython` isn't being used). However, the `Args.jobnum` property in `powerline/bindings/zsh/__init__.py ` has a type of `str`. Since `'0' != 0`, the segment is shown.

### Solution
Update the `Args.jobnum` property to convert the type to `int`.

As I identified the cause and solution, I will make the changes and submit a pull request.

issue_title: Proposal: Add SchemaMeta.process_meta hook to SchemaMeta.__new__
issue_body:
I have a need in [justanr/marshmallow-annotations](https://github.com/justanr/marshmallow-annotations) to process the Schema's meta definition before gathering declared fields, somewhere in this code block:

https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/blob/1781e9a18895aa9b473fb5587de3cf922416733e/marshmallow/schema.py#L94-L101

This line:

https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/blob/1781e9a18895aa9b473fb5587de3cf922416733e/marshmallow/schema.py#L97

is *almost* what I need except it doesn't receive the schema class as part of it's arguments.

Right now I'm working around this by doing Meta related stuff in [`get_declared_fields` which is kind of gross](https://github.com/justanr/marshmallow-annotations/blob/a4e06f5d5acb714fab6584ef005d1a404e8c70bb/src/marshmallow_annotations/scheme.py#L22-L35):

```python
        meta = getattr(klass, "Meta", None)

        if not meta:  # pragma: no branch
            return fields

        target = getattr(meta, "target", None)

        if target is None:
            return fields

        # need to walk up parents to find if they have a custom converter
        converter_factory = getattr(meta, "converter", BaseConverter)

        # weeeee circular references!
        meta.converter = converter = converter_factory(klass)
```

This hook would also relieve the circular reference as the converter only needs to know about the scheme/meta instance to do traversals up to find options set in parents. Instead with a hook like:

```python
@classmethod
def process_meta(mcls, klass, this_meta=None):
    pass
```

Meta could be processed before hand and my `get_declared_fields` becomes much simpler.

Alternatively, we could pass the schema class into the options class (at the risk of breaking other custom implementations).

Something else I've briefly considered is along the lines of:

```python
class SomeSchema(Schema):
    @classmethod
    def OPTIONS_CLASS(cls, meta, ordered):
        return CustomOpts(meta, ordered, cls)
```

but that seems a bit TOO clever.

issue_body:
We cannot adopt -Werror in part because we're called typeid on non-trivial expressions, e.g.
```

      expression with side effects will be evaluated despite being used as an
      operand to 'typeid' [-Wpotentially-evaluated-expression] 
       TS_ASSERT(typeid(*parallel_vals[i]) == typeid(*val));
                        ^
```
We should either avoid this usage or use pragmas to explicitly mark this as safe, if applicable. Then we can adopt -Werror=potentially-evaluated-expression or -Werror as appropriate

issue_title: Expose first_solar_spectral_correction in ModelChain and PVSystem
issue_body:
I suggest the following steps to expose the ``first_solar_spectral_correction`` function to ``ModelChain``:

1. Add a ``PVSystem.first_solar_spectral_correction(pw, airmass_absolute)`` method that wraps the ``atmosphere.first_solar_spectral_correction(pw, airmass_absolute, module_type=None, coefficients=None)`` function. This is straightforward except for deciding how to handle the keyword arguments. The Sandia module database has a ``Material`` key  and the CEC database has a ``Technology`` key. These keys could be mapped to the allowed ``module_type`` arguments. I'm not sure what to do about the ``coefficients`` option. Perhaps direct users to put their custom coefficients in ``module_parameters['fs_spectral_coefficients']``?

2. Implement the stub ``ModelChain.first_solar_spectral_loss`` method. Maybe something like:
```python
    def first_solar_spectral_loss(self):
        self.spectral_modifier = self.system.first_solar_spectral_loss(
            self.weather['precipitable_water'], self.airmass['airmass_absolute'])
        return self
``` 
I'm guessing that most users don't have PW in their weather dataframes, so this line is going to be a frequent source of errors. We might consider adding a ``try/except KeyError`` with a more helpful error message.

3. Improve ``ModelChain.infer_spectral_model``. This probably amounts to setting ``mc._spectral_model = mc.first_solar_spectral_loss`` if a CEC module or a ``module_parameters['fs_spectral_coefficients']`` key is detected on the ``PVSystem`` object.

4. Optional. Add a dewpoint temperature to precipitable water conversion to the ``ModelChain.prepare_inputs`` method.

5. Add tests.

6. Update documentation.

It's unfortunate that ``sapm_spectral_loss`` and ``first_solar_spectral_correction`` live in different modules. Fixing that could be step 0, or we could leave it alone.

I'm in no rush to implement this, but I'd help anyone with a pull request.

issue_title: ResourceRelatedMethodField which is rendered to relationships and works like SerializerMethodField
issue_body:
I've run into a need where i need to use `SerializerMethodField` but need to render it into `relationships` finally.
Right now, I'm using a hook in the `JSONRenderer` to move the field into `relationships` from `attributes`.
The framework accepts all other relation types as attributes  [except these](https://github.com/django-json-api/django-rest-framework-json-api/blob/develop/rest_framework_json_api/renderers.py#L52)

`ResourceRelatedField` is too restrictive as it does not allow me to modify the retrieval process.

A way to be able to to this is a `SerializerMethodField` like way to fetch data , and then render it as relationship.

A `ResourceRelatedMethodField` would be a nice solution.

issue_title: Eulers Formula
issue_body:
Hi,

symengine seems not to recognize eulers formula as in e**(ix) = cos(x) + i*sin(x), or cos(x) = (e**(i*x) + e**(ix))/2. The following code should give zero, but it doesn't. 

Analytically in my calculations the complex parts would disappear, here unfortunately symengine doesn't see this.
  
```
from symengine import I 
import symengine as si
r =si.symarray('r', 1)

b =  (si.exp(1.0*I*r[0]) + si.exp(-1.0*I*r[0]))/2 - si.cos(r[0])
print(b)
```
This gives the following
```
(1/2)*(exp((-0.0 - 1.0*I)*r_0) + exp((0.0 + 1.0*I)*r_0)) - cos(r_0)
```

I can go around this by taking the absolute value later on, but maybe there is a better solution. 

Thanks for the help!
BlackTea

issue_title: MP4: add offset of moov atom to MP4Info
issue_body:
Originally reported by: **Christoph Reiter (Bitbucket: [lazka](http://bitbucket.org/lazka), GitHub: [lazka](http://github.com/lazka))**

----------------------------------------

_From [chris.vigelius](https://code.google.com/u/chris.vigelius/) on January 11, 2011 16:54:09_
    
    When using HTTP to (pseudo-)stream MP4 files, it is important that the moov atom is at the beginning of the file, and not at the end (otherwise the player must download the whole file before it can be played).
    
    It would be very helpful if mutagen could be used to find out whether the moov position is correct or not. Fortunately that info is already available, it is just not stored.
    
    The attached patch adds a new value moov_offset to MP4Info, so that it can be retrieved by the caller.

issue_title: Consider using PhantomReference instead of finalizers for Java interface
issue_body:
Hi All,

Z3 uses the `finalize()` method to add the objects to the queue, on which `dec_ref` needs to be eventually called.
While using finalizers feels natural, in our experience (while developing CPAchecker), we have found them to cause rather severe performance issues, both with time and memory (those are outlined in Effective Java, also a relevant article is http://resources.ej-technologies.com/jprofiler/help/doc/index.html).
In addition, because finalizers run on the other thread, such approach requires locking.

We have found that it is much more safe to use `PhantomReference` instead: a special reference, which does not increase the reference count of the object for GC purposes, but gets added to the queue once the referenced object is released by the GC.
An example of such usage is https://github.com/dbeyer/cpachecker/blob/trunk/src/org/sosy_lab/cpachecker/util/predicates/bdd/JavaBDDRegionManager.java: note the `ReferenceQueue`, and the map used to retrieve the stored pointer.
The `#poll` method is used on the map, which does not lock until the data is available (it does lock then, but for a very short period of time).

What would be your thoughts on the proposal?
I can do the pull request for the migration.

We currently do not have benchmarks data for phantom references vs. finalizers for Z3 specifically, but it did help tremendously in similar circumstances (native objects which required explicit free-ing).

issue_title: bv2int and bv2nat
issue_body:
Hi,

The bitvector theory describes bv2nat, however Z3 implements bv2int. I was surprised to find out that this actually behaves like bv2nat (i.e. never produces negative numbers). Could it be renamed so that we (SPARK) don't have to generate different VCs for CVC4 and Z3?

Or, even better, support both?

(declare-fun x () Real)

z3 -smt2 file

result:

sat
((x 1.0)
 (a 1.0))

which means it finds a solution, but not the set of all solutions which would be something like `((0 < x) (a > 0))`. Do I have to specify something and, if so, why?

issue_title: What is the reason behind this timeout for a sequential array script?
issue_body:
I'm trying to get a solid understanding of arrays and quantifiers. I am playing with methods of creating an array that ascends in values. I am aware of other methods I can use to achieve this result, but that is not explaining why Z3 is struggling with this.

This works fine and is satisfiable ( [http://rise4fun.com/Z3/19Es](http://rise4fun.com/Z3/19Es) ), and creates an array where all values are equal:

```
(declare-fun a1 () (Array Int Int))
(assert (forall ((i Int)) (= (select a1 i) (select a1 (+ i 1)))))

(check-sat)
(get-model)
```

Changing the assert quantifier to say that a particular index is equal to the next index -1 causes Z3 to timeout ([http://rise4fun.com/Z3/7joWk](http://rise4fun.com/Z3/7joWk)):

```
(declare-fun a1 () (Array Int Int))
(assert (forall ((i Int)) (= (select a1 i) (- (select a1 (+ i 1)) 1) )))

(check-sat)
(get-model)
```

If this is not a bug, what should I be avoiding so this issue doesn't arise again?

issue_title: Validate that indicator patterns use valid observable types and property names
issue_body:
Issues like oasis-open/cti-stix2-json-schemas#55 are caused because we don't validate the contents of Indicator pattern expressions, just the syntax. We should be able to combine the [stix2patterns.inspector module](https://github.com/oasis-open/cti-pattern-validator/blob/master/stix2patterns/inspector.py) with the data in `enums.py` to check whether object types and properties (including via referenced objects) are valid.

For custom types and properties, we can follow the same rules as the rest of the validator:
- allow them regardless if they follow the correct naming conventions, and 
- raise an error if they don't, which can be ignored/silenced.

To scope this, I'll assume the following are out of scope for now:
- Checking if the values are the right type (since this isn't yet tracked in enums.py)
- Allowing users to specify names of properties of custom objects in or nested property names in custom properties. So basically, as soon as a (valid) custom object name or property name is encountered, checking that component of the pattern stops.
