issue_title: lasagne.updates.adam yields float64 updates with float32 0d-array input
issue_body:
Just tracked down this strange behavior.

```Python
>>> import theano
>>> import lasagne
>>> import numpy as np
>>> import theano.tensor as T
>>> x = theano.shared(np.ones((10,), dtype=theano.config.floatX))
>>> f = T.sum(x**2)
>>> u = lasagne.updates.adam(f, [x], beta2=.99)
>>> print [v.type for v in u.values()]
[TensorType(float32, vector),
 TensorType(float32, vector),
 TensorType(float32, vector),
 TensorType(float32, scalar)]
>>> u = lasagne.updates.adam(
...     f, [x], beta2=np.array(.99, dtype=theano.config.floatX))
>>> print [v.type for v in u.values()]
[TensorType(float32, vector),
 TensorType(float64, vector),
 TensorType(float64, vector),
 TensorType(float32, scalar)]
```

If the resulting updates are passed into `theano.function`, a type error is raised because the update for a `float32` values is `float64`.  In this particular case, what's happening is that in `lasagne.adams.updates`, the following:
```
v_t = beta2*v_prev + (1-beta2)*g_t**2
```
`(1 - beta2)` will be given a dtype `float64`:
```Python
>>> (1 - np.array(0., dtype='float32')).dtype
dtype('float64')
```

So, given this, I think we have three options:

1. Implicitly cast things using `utils.floatX` everywhere, which would make the line in question become
`beta2*v_prev + (utils.floatX(1.)-beta2)*g_t**utils.floatX(2.)` (puke)

1. Convert arguments to updates functions to `float`, because if a float is passed instead of a 0d-array with the correct dtype, everything works out fine thanks to Theano's willingness to cast Python scalars to the correct type

1. Tell users (like me) to obey the RTFM, i.e. `beta2 : float` is specified in the docstring, so if I pass anything else (like a numpy scalar), I'm going to have a bad time.

diff --git a/tox.ini b/tox.ini

+++ b/tox.ini

 envlist =
     py{26,27,py}-django{14,15,16}-pm{04,05},
     py{32,33}-django{15,16}-pm05,
-    py27-django{17,18,_master}-pm04,

+    py27-django{17,18,19,_master}-pm04,

 basepython =

issue_title: Kernel should alert+restart on failure
issue_body:
Currently in nteract code that causes kernel failure will leave the notebook with a quietly broken kernel.

To  reproduce, execute: 

``` python
import sys
from ctypes import CDLL
# This will crash a Linux or Mac system
# equivalent calls can be made on Windows

dll = 'dylib' if sys.platform == 'darwin' else 'so.6'
libc = CDLL("libc.%s" % dll) 
libc.time(-1)  # BOOM!!
```

and then try to run any subsequent code cell.

As an additional note, this bug was found by running through the [attached notebook](https://nbviewer.jupyter.org/github/jupyter/notebook/blob/master/docs/source/examples/Notebook/Running%20Code.ipynb). There are some other behaviors in there that we may like to mimic, e.g (Alt+Enter).

issue_title: Monorepo
issue_body:
In order to approach having more components available for an online version of the nteract components (in your friendly browser instead of Electron) we need to create a [monorepo](https://github.com/babel/babel/blob/master/doc/design/monorepo.md).

I'm in favor of exporting components _directly_ from nteract/nteract to assist with this so that's it's easy to work on the desktop app as a standalone in tandem with these core components. Especially since the API keeps evolving for actions, reducers, etc.

Motivation for "new wave modularity": http://www.macwright.org/2016/07/08/lerna-npm-organizations-new-wave-modularity.html


Sub-issues that make this easier for us:

* [ ] https://github.com/nteract/nteract/issues/1288

issue_title: Safe access to attributes
issue_body:
Hi.

Currently the compound attributes are updated one part at a time (eg: position, attitude...).

https://github.com/dronekit/dronekit-python/blob/master/dronekit/__init__.py#L1043-L1048

While updating those parts, the attribute is not in a 'safe' state.

https://github.com/dronekit/dronekit-python/blob/master/dronekit/__init__.py#L1669

Here 'self._pitch' could be the only one to have been updated, thus returning a wrong value.

It would be nice to replace those properties with a simple 'return self._property' and setting 'self._property' just before 'self.notify_attribute_listeners', thus ensuring at any time a coherent value.

Thanks.

issue_title: LSST Im3Shape support in Ganga
issue_body:
This issue is to hold all discussions relating to adding/improving Im3Shape support within Ganga.

Apologies this was originally a long email but I decided it's probably best put here with some formatting to have something to point back to when I push for the lsst branch to be merged in a few days.

## Intro
After meeting with @joezuntz before Easter I think I've got a clear idea of what is required for better LSST support within Ganga.
The ```im3shape``` executable is to be run over a large set of data (ultimately from lsst) with the amount of ways in which the data is to be processed limited by available CPU power available.

I'm trying to add a Ganga application which is capable of reproducing the behavior of https://github.com/joezuntz/grid-wl/commit/44459f3b258caf691076a398084ef4f589a1915a#diff-29c7845891b998a549bcaa7555c6adff whilst integrating more with the features offered by Ganga.

## Job requirements

A single instance of ```im3shape``` runs over a single input file and for a given ```rank``` and ```size``` will access a subset of the data available in this file.

### Binary distribution
In order to run the ```im3shape``` the application has to be made available on the worker node where it is to start running in addition to certain other pieces of information.
The best distribution method for this (at the moment) is via a DiracFile on the grid. This may change to be an installation on ```cvmfs``` when running over the whole dataset in the future.

The job script on the worker node has to be capable of downloading the output of ```im3shape``` binary (currently part of a tarball), and then run over the appropriate data file with the correct arguments.

Most of this will be checked and run by executing an ```lsst_execute.py``` script on the worker node which will focus on executing the following command with the following arguments.

### im3shape Configurables:

quoting https://github.com/joezuntz/grid-wl/commit/44459f3b258caf691076a398084ef4f589a1915a#diff-29c7845891b998a549bcaa7555c6adff:

im3shape is run using the following line

```
run-im3shape $DATA_LOCAL_PATH $INI $CAT $OUTPUT_LOCAL_PATH $JOB_RANK $JOB_COUNT
```

Where:

#### ```$DATA_LOCAL_PATH```
Location of input file to be processed

#### ```$INI``` ini file
The ```ini``` file which configures how ```im3shape``` is to run. This is currently not changed very often and so can be stored as a DiracFile by default but may be changed by the user into something such as a LocalFile.

#### ```$CAT```catalog
This defaults to ```all``` for the moment but in principle can be a file which is to be passed along with the job so can be configured to accept a LocalFile object.

#### ```$OUTPUT_LOCAL_PATH```
Location of the output file

#### ```$JOB_RANK```
Rank of the job between 0 and ```$SIZE-1``` which is used to split the dataset

#### ```$JOB_SIZE```
Size which is used to split the dataset

#### blacklist file
This is not very often updated but in principle can be changed at runtime and masks objects not be processed by the im3shape. Again this doesn't change too often and can probably default to some file on Dirac which can be overloaded.
The name and location is currently hard-coded into the binary.


## Job Splitting

LSST datasets consist of many files (~3000 for now in testing) with each file being being split into 5 processing jobs. It's possible that this may increase/reduce and is directly related to the length of time a job takes to run on the grid.
Given the large number of subjobs which are potentially created I think it may be optimal to consider

Currently a single job for testing would likely need access to a dataset of a single file:
``` /lsst/DES0005+0043-z-meds-y1a1-gamma.fits.fz ```
This file is the order of Gb in size and the jobs will ideally run for the maximal amount of time available on the backend (~24hr).
Each job has the ```rank``` and ```size``` information in the job script which allows the ```im3shape``` executable to know what data to access.

It's possible that in the future a single job may access any subset of the following:
```
/lsst/DES0005+0043-i-meds-y1a1-gamma.fits.fz
/lsst/DES0005+0043-r-meds-y1a1-gamma.fits.fz
/lsst/DES0005+0043-z-meds-y1a1-gamma.fits.fz
```
@joezuntz I'm assuming the result of a fit to ```ir``` is different to the results determined from fitting to both ```i``` and ```r``` separately?

I plan to write an Im3Splitter which can create ```size``` many subjobs per file in the dataset.
It's probably not the best idea to manage 15,000 subjobs although it should technically possible. In the short term I will write support for this to quickly get us to a position where we can use the new tools that are to be written.

### Proposed use of Im3Shape Tasks

I think the best solution to managing these many jobs in the future however is tasks.

I propose that the best solution is that ```1 task``` that submits ```~100 jobs```.
If the tasks system can nominally construct jobs there is no technical reason why the subjobs of _many_ jobs can't be merged into a single job at the task level.
If we have ```~100 jobs``` which each manage ```~150 subjobs``` which is made up of running over ```30``` datasets with ```5``` ranks per combination.

This allows us to do the same as submitting ```~3,000 jobs``` with each submitting only ```5 subjobs```.

@drmarkwslater Is there a minimal Task/Transform object which shows how tasks manage the flow of jobs/data? If not should I'll muddle through ```ITask``` to work out what needs implementing for simple job splitting/submitting?
(Can ```ITask``` be moved to the Adapters folder in the future?)

## Proposed Im3Shape Jobs within Ganga

A typical LSST application will be configured something similar to the following:

```
myApp = Im3Shape( location = ..., ini_location=..., blacklist=..., catalog=... )
j = Job(backend=Dirac(), application = myApp )
j.splitter = Im3Splitter( size = 5 )
j.inputdata = [ DiracFile(lfn='/lsst/DES0005+0043-i-meds-y1a1-gamma.fits.fz') ]
j.outputdata = [ DiracFile(...), DiracFile(...) ]
j.submit()
```

A lot of machinery such as handling of outputfiles and placing them in ```correct``` places should automatically be done using the machinery within Ganga.

Once it's clear how the Task should be written/configured it should be possible to get Ganga to manage and submit a large number of Dirac jobs which all have a very similar configuration over a large dataset.

## Current Todo:

### Work in Pogress
My work has currently focused on:

- [ ] Implementing Im3Shape Application
- [ ] Implementing Im3Splitter for jobs running with Im3Shape application
- [ ] Implementing Im3Shape Dirac backend support to allow for Dirac scripts to be auto-generated for the job
- [ ] Implementing support for the Local and Batch backends (may require some testing of DiracFile)
- [ ] Implement a new Im3Shape Task which would allow for managing jobs in a much easier way
- [ ] A solution for #201

### Some open points for discussion
Some discussion here is welcome:

- [ ] Do we want to support the Merger of __remote__ files (this keeps coming up)?
- [ ] DiracFile && Batch support. Aka accessing DiracFile from a batch system which can access the remote file but does not have Dirac tools available on the Node.
- [ ] Anything else?

issue_title: Cannot select multiple fields in sub tree: parent.subparent.field
issue_body:
We noticed a problem with the 'fields' filtering in recently deployed versions.

Works: Just show attachment.original.filename field
https://firefox.settings.services.mozilla.com/v1/buckets/fennec/collections/catalog/records?_fields=attachment.original.filename

Works: Just show the attachment.original.filename.hash field
https://firefox.settings.services.mozilla.com/v1/buckets/fennec/collections/catalog/records?_fields=attachment.original.hash

Does **NOT** work: Just show the attachment.original.filename and attachment.original.filename.hash fields (Only returns hash):
https://firefox.settings.services.mozilla.com/v1/buckets/fennec/collections/catalog/records?_fields=attachment.original.filename,attachment.original.hash

Does work: Just show the attachment.original.filename and attachment.location fields:
https://firefox.settings.services.mozilla.com/v1/buckets/fennec/collections/catalog/records?_fields=attachment.original.filename,attachment.location

It seems like as soon as we select more than one field in attachment.original.* just one field is returned.

issue_title: handling level=0 in waverec/wavedec functions
issue_body:
I found an inconsistency in all `wavedec` functions relative to their `waverec` counterparts.  If the signal is too small for the chosen wavelet, `level` will be set to 0 in `wavedec` and a length 1 `coeffs_list` containing only the original data will be returned.  If this is then passed in to `waverec`, an error will occur, because it only allows coefficient lists of length >= 2.

There are two potential solutions here:
1.) raise an error in `_check_level` if level = 0.
2.) modify `waverecn` to allow length 1 coefficient arrays and just return `coeffs[0]` (which is the original data) if the coeffs input was length 1. 

I would lean toward solution 2, possibly adding a warning in `wavedecn` and `waverecn` if level=0.

issue_title: Memory leak
issue_body:
It looks like pywt has a memory leak. When I process more than 200K small images (<10 KB each) a process takes all 16GB of my memory and stops (or slows down).

1. Code - whash() function from imagehash lib:
https://github.com/JohannesBuchner/imagehash/pull/23/commits/da9386da76d3d32f6ba41160c7ef284102df36fc

Reproduction code snippet:
res = {}
i = 0
for filename in onlyfiles:
    fname = os.path.join(filedir, filename)
    try:
        image = PIL.Image.open(fname)
        whash = imagehash.whash(image)
        value = whash.hash.flatten()
        res[int(filename[:-4])] = sum(1<<i for i, b in enumerate(value) if b)
    except Exception as e:
        print("Error. File " + fname + ": ", e )

Btw.. imagehash.phash() function doesn't use pywt and does not have this issue.

issue_title: Dealing with cursors in ConnectionField resolve functions
issue_body:
In the Star Wars relay example, it looks like you expect the `resolve_ships` method of the `Faction` class to return every ship. While this is feasible with a small dataset like that, it's not an assumption you can make. Does the resolve function get access to the `first` and `after` argument? Also, how do we supply pagination info?

Looking at the code, it looks like `connection_from_list` deals with the resolved data as a complete list. Is there a way to get around this? If the resolve function only gets the arguments, but no way to supply pagination info, should we just return 1 more object than was requested, so that `connection_from_list` knows there's more and can correctly create the pagination info?

to show in this how you can create a simple GraphQL schema in not too much time.
Then we can add additional sections, like:

- [ ] Authentication
- [ ] Permission
- [ ] Caching / Data Loaders

If you have any addition topic that should be covered in the tutorial let me know :)

Also, if you think there's a better example (the polls app is quite simple) let me know as well.

I plan to work on this in the next few weeks :)

issue_title: Updates to RxPY (vNext)
issue_body:
As a heads up, I was talking with the maintainer of RxPY and there will be major changes to the library by year's end. This major change will be both a refactor of RxPY and will also support the notion of an `AsyncObservable`, bridged from the `aioreactive` library: [RxPy vNext](https://github.com/ReactiveX/RxPY/tree/vnext).

I think the major benefit to `graphql-core` will be that we could remove the [`AsyncgenObservable`](https://github.com/graphql-python/graphql-core/blob/master/graphql/execution/executors/asyncio_utils.py#L55) as this should be natively supported (from [`aioreactive`](https://github.com/dbrattli/aioreactive#asynchronous-iteration)).

Forbidden (403)

Help

Reason given for failure:

    CSRF token missing or incorrect.

In general, this can occur when there is a genuine Cross Site Request Forgery, or when Django's CSRF mechanism has not been used correctly. For POST forms, you need to ensure:

  • Your browser is accepting cookies.
  • The view function passes a request to the template's render method.
  • In the template, there is a {% csrf_token %} template tag inside each POST form that targets an internal URL.
  • If you are not using CsrfViewMiddleware, then you must use csrf_protect on any views that use the csrf_token template tag, as well as those that accept the POST data.
  • The form has a valid CSRF token. After logging in in another browser tab or hitting the back button after a login, you may need to reload the page with the form, because the token is rotated after a login.

You're seeing the help section of this page because you have DEBUG = True in your Django settings file. Change that to False, and only the initial error message will be displayed.

You can customize this page using the CSRF_FAILURE_VIEW setting.

issue_title: Ignore outliers setting can hide entire series
issue_body: y axis is not automatically adjusted (and cannot be manually adjusted either) to fit multiple runs data series on one plot. Example: [[files to reproduce](https://s3-eu-west-1.amazonaws.com/adamg-files/logdir.zip)] There are 2 runs (`train` and `validation`) and both contain the same variable `tower_0/costs/error`. Till step ~2.5k, when both runs are selected, you can only see `train` run data. It seems to me this is a bug, I would expect the y axis to be automatically adjusted to see both runs data series. The tooltip also doesn't show `validation` run data when I inspect the graph. However the `validation` run data is there and shows up once I deselect the 'train' run. (Pay attention to the y axis values between plots.) Only later once the `validation` error drops below initial `train` error you can see both runs at the same time, but you still cannot zoom out to see both data series in the entire range of x axis. That is certainly a missing feature. Information which may be relevant: -run `train` starts logging first in terms of steps and time -run `validation` starts running and logging at arbitrary step, i.e. step count doesn't start at 0 I think this may be relevant to how tensorboard decides what axis limits to plot. All in all it's an annoying bug, because I thought that the `validation` summaries are just not saved since I could not see them, and it took a while to realize they actually are saved, just not plotted. I'm using Tensorflow 1.2.0-rc0

issue_title: Possible childID duplication
issue_body:
In MleRouter::SendChildIdResponse child ID is chosen as mNextChildId, which is just a loop-over counter from 1 to 511. Because no checks are done whether the ID is already assigned to existing child, there exists a very low probability that a new child will be assigned with a duplicate child ID and therefore short address.
This could potentially lead to malicious exploitation.

issue_title: Extend a driver and reference it with napalm-ansible
issue_body:
As discussed in [115](https://github.com/napalm-automation/napalm-ansible/issues/115) I am bringing this topic to a broader audience. 

I am trying to write some custom methods for my project; e.g. get_service_policies(). 

I have created in my working directory the **custom_napalm/** folder with **ios.py** and **__init__.py** ; ios.py extends the IOSDriver adding the desired method as explained in [doc]( http://napalm.readthedocs.io/en/latest/tutorials/extend_driver.html)

Everything works fine when using this method from the main python script (run in the working directory).

What I am struggling to achieve is to refer this method in some ansible playbook using the napalm-ansible module. 

I understand the task has to be written as follows:

```
- name: get facts from device
  connection : local
  napalm_get_facts:
    hostname: "localhost"
    optional_args: {'port' : "{{local_port}}" }
    username: "{{ ansible_user }}"
    dev_os: "{{ os }}"
    password: "{{ ansible_password }}"
    filter: ["service_policies"]
  register: result
```

The problem is that the ansible module napalm_get_facts.py is executed in some tmp folder without the custom_napalm folder and that's why is not possible to find ios.py.

If I add directly the method to the napalm/ios.py file it works from the ansible module as well.

issue_title: Generar método intermedio que devuelva el reporte de datasets como objeto de python y no como reporte en un archivo
Wrappers para push_dataset_to_ckan()
issue_body:
El objetivo sería implementar wrappers que resumen formas de utilizar la función `push_dataset_to_ckan()` muy comunes o con cierta especificidad, usando los parámetros opcionales de la función o iterándola.

- [x] `restore_dataset_to_ckan()`: restaura un dataset con su metadata *idéntica*, de un data.json a un CKAN.
- [x] `harvest_dataset_to_ckan()`: federa un dataset de un nodo original (data.json) a uno indexador (CKAN), transformando algunos campos de metadatos en el camino.
- [ ] `restore_catalog_to_ckan()`: tiene un parámetro opcional que es un array de identifiers de datasets, para itera la función `restore_dataset_to_ckan()`. Si es `None` esta función restaura *todos* los datasets del catálogo.
- [x] `harvest_catalog_to_ckan()`: tiene un parámetro opcional que es un array de identifiers de datasets, para itera la función `harvest_dataset_to_ckan()`. Si es `None` esta función federa *todos* los datasets del catálogo.

issue_title: model.predict() modifies input (bug?)
issue_body:
With an up-to-date TFLearn (0.3.2) the DNN model.predict() function appears to modify its input (here the CIFAR-10 test-image set):

```python
n_neg = np.count_nonzero(testX < 0)
print("before: ",n_neg)

testX_copy = np.copy(testX)
predY = model.predict(testX)
with open("/results/test_predictions.dat","wb") as f:
  pickle.dump(predY,f)
  
n_neg = np.count_nonzero(testX < 0)
print("after: ",n_neg)

print("nope: ",np.count_nonzero(testX == testX_copy))
```

Output:
```
before:  0
after:  15940587
nope:  0
```

I'm pretty sure this wasn't the case a while ago (can't give a specific version unfortunately), because I only noticed it because I was directly feeding the input (after predict) to matplotlib's imshow(), but that stopped working, complaining the input wasn't in range [0 .. 1].  Modifying to feed predict an np.copy() of testX makes things work again, but I wanted to bring it up here in case this is indeed a bug.

issue_title: Pretrained russian vector has different length size
Question: can fastText capture the structure of a text (i.e. poem metrics)?
issue_body:
Supposed to have a lyrics poem, like `Elizabeth Barrett Browning`'s famous `How Do I Love Thee`

```
How do I love thee? Let me count the ways.

I love thee to the depth and breadth and height

My soul can reach, when feeling out of sight

For the ends of Being and ideal Grace

I love thee to the level of everyday's

Most quiet need, by sun and candle-light.

I love thee freely, as men strive for Right;

I love thee purely, as they turn from Praise.

I love thee with the passion put to use

In my old griefs, and with my childhood's faith

I love thee with a love I seem to love

With my lost saints, - I love thee with the breath,

Smiles, tears, of all my life! - and, if God choose,

I shall but love thee better after death.
```

If the input dataset has a normalized text (like removing diacritics, none [a-zA-Z] chars, etc.) but keeping `\n` or `\r\n`,  therefore it keep the metrics of the poem, will the network be able to classify it?

At this time my normalization looks like [here](https://github.com/loretoparisi/fasttext.js/blob/master/lib/index.js#L312)

```javascript
/**
 * Normalize test helper
 * @see https://github.com/facebookresearch/fastText/blob/master/classification-example.sh#L15
 * @param text string
 * @return string
 */
FastText.normalize = function(text) {
    text=text.toLowerCase()
    .replace(/^/gm, '__label__')
    .replace(/(?:\\[rn]|[\r\n]+)+/g, " ")
    .replace(/'/g, " ' ")
    .replace(/"/g, '')
    .replace(/\./g, ' \. ')
    .replace(/,/g, ' \, ')
    .replace(/\(/g, ' ( ')
    .replace(/\)/g, ' ) ')
    .replace(/!/g, ' ! ')
    .replace(/\?/g, ' ! ')
    .replace(/;/g, ' ')
    .replace(/:/g, ' ')
    .replace(/\t+/g,'\t').replace(/\t\s/g,' ').replace(/\t/g,' ');
    return text;
```


If not, is this more likely a `LSTM` task for [fairseq](https://github.com/facebookresearch/fairseq)?
My guess is about the text tokenizer that could skip the carriage return anyways...
Thank you.

issue_title: Utility functions for importing key files on disk?
issue_body:
A pretty standard use of this library is dealing with key files which need to be serialized/deserialized to disk. The JWK objects should either expose a constructor which can initialize them from on-disk PEM files, or else maybe some utility functionality should be provided to make this available?

A good middle-ground would be allowing directly initializing JWK's from rsa.Cryptography objects, since internally this is done anyway with functions like `JWK._rsa_pri`

issue_title: Refactor Cmd2.parseline()
issue_body:
To ensure consistency in parsing, we need to do a bit more refactoring:

- refactor tab completion to use StatementParser instead of parseline()
- refactor Cmd2.parseline() to use StatementParser.parse()

These refactoring tasks involve a complex issue related to submenu support, namely submenu instances don't have access to the `self.command_parser` attribute.

issue_title: Improve CODEOWNERS file
issue_body:
Over time as more people have gotten involved with this project and more features have gotten added, certain people have become the "expert" at certain parts of the codebase.  It no longer makes sense for me to be the sole owner of all of the code in the CODEOWNERS file.  

Getting nominated as an "owner" in this file automatically nominates you as a reviewer for a PR in which this file changes.  

I would like to update this file to more accurately reflect who best understands which pieces of code.

issue_title: Explore automatically paging long output
issue_body:
It would be nice if long output which is bigger than can fit within the height of the terminal is automatically paged or at least if the option for doing so in an opt-in fashion existed within ``cmd2``.

The size of the terminal can be retrieved on all platforms as follows:
```Python
import os
height, width = os.get_terminal_size()
```

It probably wouldn't make sense to add a dependency on [Click](http://click.pocoo.org) just for this, but Click's [echo_via_pager()](http://click.pocoo.org/5/utils/#pager-support) function provides nice pager support.  Perhaps we could use the [underlying implementation](https://github.com/pallets/click/blob/master/click/_termui_impl.py) for Click's pager support as an inspiration.

issue_title: Starter stuck when job failed/executed too quickly
issue_body:
Consider the example when job has failed too quickly (for instance, due to the invalid parameter value), in such case job status will be changed to `FAILED` and stuck forever (with running processes). It will similarly stuck when job is successfully finished. 

To replicate use the following script and run it with starter `aetros run --local`:
```python
import aetros.backend

if __name__ == '__main__':
    job = aetros.backend.context()
    raise IOError("File not found")
```
However, if you run it with `python quick.py` it will finish properly (i.e. status will be changed to `STOPPED`), which suggests that the problem is with aetros starter.
Interestingly, that when you run it in debug mode `DEBUG=1 aetros run --local` or add delay `sleep(1)` after job initialisation, it will also finish properly. Might be something related to race conditions / async issues (i.e. when job hasn't initialised properly but already finished)

issue_title: Embedding instructions don't include compilation flags when following a regular module
issue_body:
If I build both a regular module and an embedded target the c++ standard doesn't get added to the compilation flags:

```CMake
cmake_minimum_required(VERSION 3.0)
project(example)
add_subdirectory(pybind11)
pybind11_add_module(example example_pybind11.cpp)
add_executable(main main.cpp)
target_link_libraries(main PRIVATE pybind11::embed)
```

Compilation lines (extracted from `make VERBOSE=1`):
```
/usr/bin/c++   -Dexample_EXPORTS -I/home/jagerman/src/pybind11/scratch/tasty/pybind11/include -I/usr/include/python3.6m  -fPIC   -std=c++14 -flto -fno-fat-lto-objects -o CMakeFiles/example.dir/example_pybind11.cpp.o -c /home/jagerman/src/pybind11/scratch/tasty/example_pybind11.cpp
```
```
/usr/bin/c++    -I/home/jagerman/src/pybind11/scratch/tasty/pybind11/include -I/usr/include/python3.6m   -o CMakeFiles/main.dir/main.cpp.o -c /home/jagerman/src/pybind11/scratch/tasty/main.cpp
```

If I comment out the `pybind11_add_module` the main.cpp compilation line becomes:
```
/usr/bin/c++    -I/home/jagerman/src/pybind11/scratch/tasty/pybind11/include -I/usr/include/python3.6m  -std=c++14 -o CMakeFiles/main.dir/main.cpp.o -c /home/jagerman/src/pybind11/scratch/tasty/main.cpp
```

issue_title: Default named arguments for init functions
issue_body:
I'm working on converting a [project](https://github.com/GalSim-developers/GalSim) from Boost Python to PyBind11, mostly to try to make it pip installable, since the Boost dependency is kind of a stopper for that.

So far, one thing that boost.python does nicely that I can't figure out how to do easily in pybind11 is to give default values to the arguments of an `__init__` method.  

For instance, here is the code to wrap one of our classes using boost::python:
```
namespace bp = boost::python;
bp::class_<GaussianDeviate, bp::bases<BaseDeviate> >
        pyGaussianDeviate("GaussianDeviate", "", bp::no_init);
pyGaussianDeviate
    .def(bp::init<long, double, double>(
            (bp::arg("seed")=0, bp::arg("mean")=0., bp::arg("sigma")=1.)
    ))
```
(There's more than this of course, but this is the relevant bit.)

So far, my attempt to convert this to pybind11 API involves:
* change namespace to pybind11 (of course)
* remove `no_init`
* remove `bases` wrapper for `BaseDeviate`
* remove the parentheses around the args

which leads to
```
namespace bp = pybind11;
bp::class_<GaussianDeviate, BaseDeviate>
        pyGaussianDeviate("GaussianDeviate");
pyGaussianDeviate
    .def(bp::init<long, double, double>(
            bp::arg("seed")=0, bp::arg("mean")=0., bp::arg("sigma")=1.
    ))
```
However, this is still insufficient.  I get the following error from the compiler (using pybind11 2.0.0rc1)
```
pysrc/Random.cpp:143:40: error: use of overloaded operator '=' is ambiguous (with operand types
      'bp::arg' and 'int')
                        bp::arg("seed")=0, bp::arg("mean")=0., bp::arg("sigma")=1.
                        ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~^~
/Users/Mike/Library/Python/2.7/include/python2.7/pybind11/attr.h:20:8: note: candidate is the
      implicit move assignment operator
struct arg {
       ^
/Users/Mike/Library/Python/2.7/include/python2.7/pybind11/attr.h:20:8: note: candidate is the
      implicit copy assignment operator
/Users/Mike/Library/Python/2.7/include/python2.7/pybind11/attr.h:24:24: note: candidate function
      [with T = int]
    constexpr arg_t<T> operator=(const T &value) const { return {name, value}; }
                       ^
```
Can you please tell me the correct syntax to do this in pybind11?  I couldn't find anything in the docs about constructors with default arguments.  The above kind of syntax (`bp::arg('name') = value`) works for other functions.  Just not `init`.

I think I could do this by writing a custom initialization function that I wrap as
```
    .def("__init__", &CustomGaussianDeviateInit,
            bp::arg("seed")=0, bp::arg("mean")=0., bp::arg("sigma")=1.
    ))
```
But that's a lot of extra boiler plate that I would hope I don't have to write.  (There are quite a few classes that we do this kind of thing for.)  So if this is the only current solution, please consider this issue a feature request to enable the old syntax that boost python had for this (or something similar).

Thanks!

issue_title: Uploading of test results should be done after the test run
issue_body:
So far, I have not been able to use `pytest-reportportal` agent successfully. After thorough investigation, I noticed that our testsuite uses `patch` in a few cases for `builtin.open` and `requests` library. When this is done, it has an impact on the workings of the async RP service object which pushes results to the server.

To work around this, pushing the results should only be done after the test suite has ran and all the `patch` setups are cleaned up.

issue_title: [google-calendar] Failed to download event (because of escaping?)
issue_labels: [{'id': 336203794, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWwzMzYyMDM3OTQ=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/pimutils/vdirsyncer/labels/in%20progress', 'name': 'in progress', 'color': 'ededed', 'default': False}, {'id': 353106092, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWwzNTMxMDYwOTI=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/pimutils/vdirsyncer/labels/mod:%20Google', 'name': 'mod: Google', 'color': '1d76db', 'default': False}, {'id': 80748616, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWw4MDc0ODYxNg==', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/pimutils/vdirsyncer/labels/type:%20bug', 'name': 'type: bug', 'color': 'eb6420', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
```
› vdirsyncer --version
vdirsyncer, version 0.16.4
› python --version
Python 3.6.5
```

Running on Arch Linux, syncing to Google Calendar via CalDAV.

---

I get sync errors for a single entry in the calendar. It looks like it has to do with the automatic URL escaping of `href`s.

## Error

I've tried to extract relevant lines out of the `-vdebug` output; it contains thousands and thousands of lines.

```
debug:  <D:response xmlns:carddav="urn:ietf:params:xml:ns:carddav" xmlns:cm="http://cal.me.com/_namespace/" xmlns:md="urn:mobileme:davservi
ces">
debug:   <D:href>/caldav/v2/magnus.bergmark%40gmail.com/events/2012-08-07+08%3A42_320847479%40napp01.ics</D:href>
debug:   <D:propstat>
debug:    <D:status>HTTP/1.1 200 OK</D:status>
debug:    <D:prop>
debug:     <D:getcontenttype>text/calendar; component=vevent</D:getcontenttype>
debug:     <D:getetag>"63480004950"</D:getetag>
debug:     <D:resourcetype/>
debug:    </D:prop>
debug:   </D:propstat>
debug:  </D:response>

debug: Normalized URL from '/caldav/v2/magnus.bergmark%40gmail.com/events/2012-08-07+08%3A42_320847479%40napp01.ics' to '/caldav/v2/magnus.bergmark@gmail.com/events/2012-08-07%2B08:42_320847479@napp01.ics'

debug: Already normalized: '/caldav/v2/magnus.bergmark@gmail.com/events/2012-08-07%2B08:42_320847479@napp01.ics'

debug: <D:href>/caldav/v2/magnus.bergmark@gmail.com/events/2012-08-07%2B08:42_320847479@napp01.ics</D:href>
debug:  <D:response xmlns:carddav="urn:ietf:params:xml:ns:carddav" xmlns:cm="http://cal.me.com/_namespace/" xmlns:md="urn:mobileme:davservi
ces">
debug:   <D:href>/caldav/v2/magnus.bergmark@gmail.com/events/2012-08-07%2B08:42_320847479@napp01.ics</D:href>
debug:   <D:status>HTTP/1.1 404 Not Found</D:status>
debug:  </D:response>
debug: </D:multistatus>
```

## Debugging

I suspected that the problem had to do with the normalization of the `href` so I tried to patch the code at runtime to check.

```python
def _normalize_href(base, href):
    '''Normalize the href to be a path only relative to hostname and
    schema.'''
    orig_href = href
    if not href:
        raise ValueError(href)

    x = urlparse.urljoin(base, href)
    x = urlparse.urlsplit(x).path

    # Encoding issues:
    # - https://github.com/owncloud/contacts/issues/581
    # - https://github.com/Kozea/Radicale/issues/298
    old_x = None
    while old_x is None or x != old_x:
        if _contains_quoted_reserved_chars(x):
            break
        old_x = x
        x = urlparse.unquote(x)

    x = urlparse.quote(x, '/@%:')


    # PATCH HERE
    if orig_href == '/caldav/v2/magnus.bergmark%40gmail.com/events/2012-08-07+08%3A42_320847479%40napp01.ics':
        dav_logger.debug('Oh boy, here we go!')
        return orig_href
    # PATCH HERE

    if orig_href == x:
        dav_logger.debug('Already normalized: {!r}'.format(x))
    else:
        dav_logger.debug('Normalized URL from {!r} to {!r}'
                         .format(orig_href, x))

    return x

```

With this change in place the sync finished successfully.

## Escaping changes

* `2012-08-07+08%3A42_320847479%40napp01.ics` (Original)
* `2012-08-07%2B08:42_320847479@napp01.ics` (Escaped)

The `+` was changed to `%2B`, the already escaped `%3A` was unescaped into `:`, and the already escaped `%40` got unescaped to `@`.

I changed the debug code above to not return `orig_href`, but a string with only one out of the three changes applied. It does not matter which one I do (`+` -> `%2B`, `%3A` -> `:`, `%40` -> `@`), it started to fail again.

So it seems like this `href` that is returned from the Google CalDAV API must not be changed at all or their server will not find it.

Is there something else I can provide you or help with?

issue_title: add the word `instead` to renew error
issue_body:
I tried to renew a certificate:

```sh
# ~/certbot-auto -d server.example.com renew
Saving debug log to /var/log/letsencrypt/letsencrypt.log
Currently, the renew verb is capable of either renewing all installed certificates that are due to be renewed or renewing a single certificate specified by its name. If you would like to renew specific certificates by their domains, use the certonly command. The renew verb may provide other options for selecting certificates to renew in the future.
```

I'm not sure why my original command was rejected, I only specified one domain.
So, I naively tried to fix my command:
```sh
# ~/certbot-auto certonly -d server.example.com renew
usage:
  certbot-auto [SUBCOMMAND] [options] [-d DOMAIN] [-d DOMAIN] ...

Certbot can obtain and install HTTPS/TLS/SSL certificates.  By default,
it will attempt to use a webserver both for obtaining and installing the


cert.


certbot: error: unrecognized arguments: certonly
```

Suggestions:
1. Accept the input I provided (my guess is that the order `-d foo renew` confused renew and had I used `renew -d foo` it would have been ok)
1. Change `the certonly command` to `the certonly command instead`. I'm not really sure why I think the word "instead" would have queued my brain to drop the word renew from the command, but I think it would have.

issue_body:
- Should we not save the installer when we run `certonly`?
- Should `restart` be called inside of `deploy_cert`?

issue_title: Support `certbot help <command>` syntax
issue_body:
This is a fairly common idiom in command line tools (at least in Go), and it's nice to be generous about giving help.

Right now if you run this you get the regular help, with an easy-to-miss error squished at the bottom:

```
certbot: error: unrecognized arguments: help
```

issue_title: Plugins packaging
issue_body:
Here's an idea: move all plugins to separate git repositories and distribute them in a separate PyPI/Debian/etc. packages (that is all apart from standalone, which is probably best to be left in the main distribution). setuptools entry_points system, which is already being used in `master`, makes such transformation a breeze.

| entry point name | GitHub | PyPI | Debian |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| apache | letsencrypt-apache | letsencrypt-apache | letsencrypt-apache |
| nginx | letsencrypt-nginx | letsencrypt-nginx | letsencrypt-nginx |
| ... | ... | ... | ... |
- PyPI packages would have to `install_requires.append('letsencrypt')`, as well as set up `entry_points` appropriately
- Debian plugin packages would `Require: letsencrypt` as well as specific server software, e.g. `letsecrnypt-apache` would `Require: apache`. `letsencrypt` Debian package could `Suggest: letsencrypt-apache`, etc.
- CLI would automatically populate `--{plugin_name}-{option_name}`, similarly `{plugin_name}_{option_name}` for config

This would simplify a lot of things, such as release process, Python 3 compatibility transition, OS support (e.g. AFAIR augeas, necessary for apache or nginx plugins, is not even available on Windows, what about it's py3 compat?)... Main cons is probably less control over the namespace (e.g. PyPI), or establising unique `{plugin_name}` etc.

https://github.com/kuba/lets-encrypt-preview/tree/cli brings us closer to the required architecture (moves plugin specific constants and CLI arguments to plugins subdirs, first attempt at unique plugin names problem)

cc: @jdkasten, @fmarier

issue_title: Add "Secure + HSTS" to "Easy vs Secure" prompt
issue_body:
Ok, we have in certbot-auto prompt like this (see below), we could add choice named "Secure + HSTS" that automatically installs the current "Secure" and HSTS to our webserver conf-files.

```
Please choose whether HTTPS access is required or optional.
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1: Easy - Allow both HTTP and HTTPS access to these sites
2: Secure - Make all requests redirect to secure HTTPS access
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
```

Like this:
```
Please choose whether HTTPS access is required or optional.
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1: Easy - Allow both HTTP and HTTPS access to these sites
2: Secure - Make all requests redirect to secure HTTPS access
3. Secure + HSTS - Make all requests redirect to secure HTTPS access and use HSTS
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
```
or something like that

issue_title: Use __hash__ correctly in Nginx and Apache
issue_body:
From the documentation for [\_\_hash\_\_](https://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#object.__hash__):

> If a class defines mutable objects and implements an \_\_eq\_\_() method, it should not implement \_\_hash\_\_(), since the implementation of hashable collections requires that a key’s hash value is immutable (if the object’s hash value changes, it will be in the wrong hash bucket).

We don't change vhosts after they're created often, but we do in a few places and this could lead to bugs like the ones Python is warning us about. Might be worth making these objects immutable or writing our own code to eliminate duplicates from a collection of vhosts at some point.

issue_title: Should install/enhance take --domains?
issue_body:
Proposed for the install subcommand by Peter at https://github.com/certbot/certbot/pull/5536#issuecomment-363185524. The enhance subcommand works in a similar way by making use of an existing cert so Joona is considering this behavior for that command as well.

Do we want to do this? Should the behavior be identical?

issue_title: OOM killed installer
issue_body:
The OOM killed the certbot installer (probably during python configuration) on my VPS. At one time, the VPS crashed and I had to reboot the VPS. I am using the Blueberry plan: https://www.berry.pw/

It's time to redesign certboot using less bloated software. The tool should be designed as a native C program.

issue_title: Manpage generation tries to execute commands not yet installed
issue_body:
Currently, somewhere in the depths of sphinxbuild, it is trying to invoke the letsencrypt and letsencrypt-renewer executables in order to get the help information.  However, at the time that the manpages are built for Debian, the executables have not yet been installed to the system paths.

Is it possible to invoke these executables in place in the source tree, instead of expecting them to be in the system path at the time of generation?  (That seems to be a bit chicken-and-the-egg-y to me anyway.)

issue_title: PlainReporter colouring output of forbidden-import
issue_body:
When PlainReporter reports a `forbidden-import` error, it adds colorama colouring though it's not supposed to, resulting in colouring tokens littering plain text files. 
E.g.: running `check_all(reporter=PlainReporter)` on the following code:
```python
import sys
pass
```
results in the following plain-text output (snippet):
```
. . .
    [Line 1] You may not import any modules - you imported \x1b[34msys\x1b[0m on line 1.
. . .
```
(Note the extraneous `\x1b[34m` and `\x1b[0m`.)
The problem seems to be irrelevant of the module being imported.

issue_title: Invalid Credentials exception from inside a docker container when using service account credentials
issue_body:
Hello,

We've been using the pydatalabs modules to interact with big query in an application. When running from a local machine with my personal account this works like a charm, however when using a service account from within a docker container all commands seem to fail with:

RequestException: HTTP request failed: Invalid Credentials

However when I use gcloud.bigquery I don't seem to have this problem.

In more detail I'm creating a docker image and including the head of pydatalabs in it. I'm then building this with "pip install -e ." My code is then of the form:

import datalab.bigquery as bq

table_name = "my_project:my_dataset.my_table"
bq.Table(table_name).exists()

When run I get the following:

 File "main.py", line 75, in main
    test = bq.Table(table_name).exists()
  File "/pydatalab/datalab/bigquery/_table.py", line 189, in exists
    raise e
RequestException: HTTP request failed: Invalid Credentials

I'm exporting my service account credentials to docker via a command of the form:

docker run \
         -v my_creds.json:/etc/key_file.json \
	-e GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS=/etc/key_file.json \
	my_docker_image

As far as I can tell the credentials are correct and make it into the image, also when following the above methodology both gcloud.bigquery and gcloud.pubsub work.

Thanks

Matthew

issue_title: Use most recent ENDPOINTS_SERVICE_VERSION if not provided
issue_body:
Hi, 

I'm trying to automate an endpoints + AppEngine deployment. Firstly I push to endpoints, then i have to capture the version that was created and insert that into the env_variables in the app.yaml file before deploying my application. This is a fairly clunky flow. 

There are two things I've found here that potentially improve this:

1) Have AppEngine always use the latest API version: https://cloudplatform.googleblog.com/2018/04/Cloud-Endpoints-introducing-a-new-way-to-manage-API-configuration-rollout.html

2) Have the library fetch and use the latest version automatically: https://github.com/cloudendpoints/endpoints-management-java/blob/master/endpoints-service-config/src/main/java/com/google/api/config/ServiceConfigSupplier.java#L122-L147 https://cloud.google.com/service-management/reference/rest/v1/services.configs/list

I'm uncertain of how option 1 plays into this, whether it's a requirement for option 2, but it appears that if you don't provide a version to the java library it will simply list the available configurations and use the most recent. This would be extremely useful in automatic deployments. 

Is this something we can add to the python library?

issue_title: No header in txt from mcgenerate and convertmc workflow OR newest bdo2txt
issue_body:
So, learning to use the plgrid cluster and the new tools (mcgenerate, pymchelper) I did a run of depth-dose distributions for different energies. The runs were started with a bash script and the mcgenerate command. The output is .bdo files. The collect.sh script and the convertmc command was afterwards used to collect and convert the data. 
However, the txt files are all only raw numbers without any header containing bin numbers and such information, which I have grown accustomed to. 

I also checked the output of bdo2txt on the .bdo files, which also did not contain any header. I then checked the output of bdo2txt on old .bdo files, which before resulted in txt files with headers - this time they did not. So maybe the issue is also in the latest version of bdo2txt (--version returns X.Y.Z but it was compiled at the end of 2016).

For my post-processing scripts, I very often draw information from the headers. I am aware of the option to import and work with the binaries with pymchelper without converting and I will also start playing with that. Independently however, a header also in the txt files would be nice. They can also be helpful to remember what was done.   
If bdo2txt is slowly being phased out, then I guess it would make sense only to add the feature in convertmc.

Here is an example of a header from a txt file converted from .bdo with a bdo2txt version from 1-2 years ago:

issue_title: Execution of identical tasks.
issue_body:
We have discussed this topic before but perhaps we need to revisit:

Scenario:

```
input: for_each={'i': range(100)}
task:
   simulate()
```

In this case all tasks will be identical so they will share the same ID and signature file and cause trouble during execution. This is a bug that we should fix so we should either

1. disallow the execution of multiple tasks with the same ID, or
2. silently ignore the task that share ID with an existing queued task.
3. assign a different ID for this particular scenario.

I tend to solution 3 because this is more or less what users expect.

issue_title: CombinedParameter
issue_body:
@giulioungaretti 
Taking a look at the `parameter.CombinedParameter`
The way it works now is that it settable only to a certain index of a set array.
I would argue that it would be more useful if these parameters are settable to any tuple.
Then it would also be logical to implement a get function for these parameters that return a tuple as well.
I thought that this was also the solution we discussed in the our last QCoDeS meeting.

The reason why I would like this implementation is the following.
There are two different sorts of combined parameters in my mind.
The first kind is just a collection of individual parameters combined in a vector as is implemented in QCodes Now. 

The other is a single parameter with many vector components. Setting this parameter only calls a single set function. (An example of this is the magnetic field. It has three components, but they can only be set together, not seperately.)

Then, it should be possible to get and set this parameter as any other, but instead of scalars, one would have tuples.

As for implementation. (this is just my humble oppinion)
Rather than implementing vector parameters around the `CombinedParameter`, I would like to see that the behaviour of parameters is generalized to being able to handle vecotr values.

Combining two parameters (of dimension 2 and 3 for example) should then give a parameter (let'
s call it `par`) which can be set like
`par.set([[1, 2], [9, 8, 7]])`


As a summary:
I think the problem with the current implementation of `CombinedParameter` is that it does enable native support for vector-like parameters, it only covers the (although very useful) single usecase for a path-like sweep of parameters that already exist.

issue_title: Reduce number of coverage comments on PRs to one
issue_labels: [{'id': 429586455, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/zhmcclient/python-zhmcclient/labels/area:%20devenv', 'name': 'area: devenv', 'color': '207de5', 'default': False}, {'id': 439501531, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/zhmcclient/python-zhmcclient/labels/discussion%20needed', 'name': 'discussion needed', 'color': '159818', 'default': False}, {'id': 528447033, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/zhmcclient/python-zhmcclient/labels/resolution:%20mitigated', 'name': 'resolution: mitigated', 'color': 'cccccc', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
Currently, the coveralls service posts a number of comments for the initial PR and for each update of a PR. All these comments from one "run" provide the same link.

The number of such comments for the same "run" and link is the number of successful Travis jobs. Starting with PR #172, the Travis CI runs for Python 2.7, 3.4, 3.5, 3.6, and pypy. The Travis control file invokes coveralls at the end upon success. In all five cases, the Travis run succeeds up to the point where coveralls runs. For the python x.y versions, coveralls itself also succeeds, each one resulting in a comment on the PR. For pypy, coveralls is attempted to be run, but fails with an exception, so no coverage comment is posted for that environment.

Some investigation on the coveralls.io links: The link in the little badge in each coveralls comment targets a "build" of Travis (e.g. https://coveralls.io/builds/9852638 for Travis build 820). A "build" is the set of all Travis Python environments in a particular PR. The run for each Travis Python environment within a PR is termed a "job". There are also links to coveralls.io pages for those jobs (e.g. https://coveralls.io/jobs/22316159 for Travis job 820.1 within Travis build 820). The coveralls.io page for the build also has the links for the job pages.

Bottom line, it would be sufficient to have exactly one posted coveralls comment with the link it currently has (to the page for the "build"), and not four of them.

This issue is to reduce the number of coverage comments that are posted by the coveralls service for a particular Travis build of a PR, to one.

issue_title: StravaOAuth key error
issue_body:
The methods `get_user_id` and `get_user_details` methods are both throwing exceptions due to the "`athlete`" node not being in the response dictionary when called from `rest_framework` authentication.  They both work fine on web based authentication.

I believe the solution to be remove the `response["athlete"]` portion and revert to `response["id"],` `response["email"],` etc in these functions.

For whatever reason the response from strava in web based auth includes several of the fields repeated in an `athlete` node while the REST call does not.  The fields are however present at the root in both scenarios.

Thanks for the work you've done.  Very nice to use this library!

issue_title: Object hash mismatch for Python dictionaries
issue_body:
Python dictionaries (even when the insertion order was the same) do not have a deterministic key ordering. This means that we can get an object hash mismatch for dictionaries, even though the key-value pairs are the same.

Better code example to come, but this can be seen on this [branch](https://github.com/stephanie-wang/ray/tree/put-reconstruction). Run the following command on python (v3.5):
`python test/stress_tests.py ReconstructionTests.testPut`

issue_title: ray.wait has variable latencies
issue_body:
`ray.wait()` latencies can be affected by the workload (as shown below via sleep) and copying the result back to the driver is significantly slower than a simple array copy.

Below is a mini test suite. 
```
import numpy as np
import ray
import time
from datetime import datetime
ts = lambda: datetime.now().timestamp()
ray.init(num_workers=1, num_cpus=1)

@ray.remote
def easy():
    return 1, ts()

@ray.remote
def timing():
   time.sleep(0.1)
   return 1, ts()

@ray.remote
def big(copy_inside=False):
    ret = np.random.randn(1000000)
    if copy_inside:
        micros = ts()
        a = np.array(ret, copy=True)
        microd = ts()
        print("Memcopy took %f..." % (microd - micros)) 
    return ret, ts()

def test(fn, **kwargs):
    val = [fn.remote(**kwargs)]
    d, _ = ray.wait(val)
    done = ts()
    return done - ray.get(d)[-1][-1]
v = [test(easy) for _ in range(100)]
print ("Easy takes %f on average | %f std" % (np.mean(v), np.std(v)))
v = [test(timing) for _ in range(100)]
print ("Waiting takes %f on average | %f std" % (np.mean(v), np.std(v)))
v = [test(big, copy_inside=False) for _ in range(100)]
print ("Big takes %f on average | %f std" % (np.mean(v), np.std(v)))
```


Typical results are the following: 

Easy takes 0.000617 on average | 0.000063 std
Waiting takes 0.000975 on average | 0.000056 std
Big takes 0.021029 on average | 0.001018 std
(Copying in-function usually takes 0.0009 seconds)

issue_title: [tune] Suggestion about Hyperband
issue_body:
We have been very enthusiastic about using Hyperband or PBT implementations of Raytune for hyper-parameter optimization. But we have encountered some obstacles while employing them.

- The raytune trainable function doesn't unfortunately supports the ray remote function.
- PBT can only tune the parameters that won't affect the total number of parameters.
- Hyperband and PBT can only tune less than 10 parameters, whilst TPE can tune hundreds
- Raytune has TPE interface but that doesn't support the nested definition of the search space

We see that hyperband best suits tuning categorical parameters such as the network architecture whilst PBT rather suits tuning continous numerical variables. I am curious if Raytune can enable us to bring the best of both worlds. There are several work in literature that combines Hyperband with Bayesian optimization. Hence, I would welcome if you can enlighten me about how we can do that with Raytune and would be glad if you can add that to framework as a functionality or an example.

- "Combination of Hyperband and Bayesian Optimization for Hyperparameter Optimization in Deep Learning" https://arxiv.org/pdf/1801.01596.pdf
- "Combining Hyperband and Bayesian Optimization" 
https://bayesopt.github.io/papers/2017/36.pdf 
- BOHB: Robust and Efficient Hyperparameter Optimization at Scale
https://arxiv.org/pdf/1807.01774.pdf 
- Practical Hyperparameter Optimization for Deep Learning
https://openreview.net/forum?id=HJMudFkDf

issue_title: Support multi-flow transfers
issue_body:
AWS instances support 25 Gbps between instances in placement group, which requires opening connections to different ports. Connection between same pair of ports is limited to 10 Gbps. IE

```
172.31.1.10:40000 -> 172.31.1.11:5000 -> limited to 10 Gbps.
172.31.1.10:40001 -> 172.31.1.11:5000 -> limited to 10 Gbps.

```

To get this bandwidth out of Ray, it would need to support multiple ports. IE, if I send send 3 large objects, it would create 3 different socket connections from different ports.

The multi-flow with 3 flows can be tested by setting `-p 3` using instructions below
https://aws.amazon.com/premiumsupport/knowledge-center/network-throughput-benchmark-linux-ec2/

issue_title: numpy array is not JSON serializable
issue_body:
### System information
- **OS Platform and Distribution (e.g., Linux Ubuntu 16.04)**: Docker nvidia/cuda:9.0-base-ubuntu16.04
- **Ray installed from (source or binary)**: Binary
- **Ray version**: ray==0.4.0
- **Python version**: Python 3.5.2
- **Exact command to reproduce**:
Ray tune config with numpy arrays:
`
'config': {'var:' np.array([1.0, 2.0])}
`

### Describe the problem
Ray tune tries to convert the numpy array to JSON and fails.

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "experiments.py", line 245, in <module>
    tune.run_experiments({'pbt_modularity': train_spec}, scheduler=pbt)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/dist-packages/ray/tune/tune.py", line 63, in run_experiments
    for trial in generate_trials(spec, name):
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/dist-packages/ray/tune/variant_generator.py", line 48, in generate_trials
    args = parser.parse_args(to_argv(spec))
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/dist-packages/ray/tune/variant_generator.py", line 20, in to_argv
    argv.append(json.dumps(v))
  File "/usr/lib/python3.5/json/__init__.py", line 230, in dumps
    return _default_encoder.encode(obj)
  File "/usr/lib/python3.5/json/encoder.py", line 198, in encode
    chunks = self.iterencode(o, _one_shot=True)
  File "/usr/lib/python3.5/json/encoder.py", line 256, in iterencode
    return _iterencode(o, 0)
  File "/usr/lib/python3.5/json/encoder.py", line 179, in default
    raise TypeError(repr(o) + " is not JSON serializable")
TypeError: array([1.61153255e-05, 2.11210111e-05, 9.83538792e-06]) is not JSON serializable
```

The conversion to JSON happens here (variant_generator.py line 41 and following):
```
            try:
                # Special case the `env` param for RLlib by automatically
                # moving it into the `config` section.
                if "env" in spec:
                    spec["config"] = spec.get("config", {})
                    spec["config"]["env"] = spec["env"]
                    del spec["env"]
                args = parser.parse_args(to_argv(spec))
            except SystemExit:
                raise TuneError("Error parsing args, see above message", spec)
            if resolved_vars:
                experiment_tag = "{}_{}".format(i, resolved_vars)
            else:
                experiment_tag = str(i)
            i += 1
            yield Trial(
                trainable_name=spec["run"],
                config=spec.get("config", {}),
                local_dir=os.path.join(args.local_dir, output_path),
                experiment_tag=experiment_tag,
                resources=json_to_resources(spec.get("trial_resources", {})),
                stopping_criterion=spec.get("stop", {}),

                restore_path=spec.get("restore"),
                upload_dir=args.upload_dir,
                max_failures=args.max_failures)
```

Relevant line: `  args = parser.parse_args(to_argv(spec))`

I don't see why this conversion is necessary in the first place.
Because there are only few fields of the spec (args.upload_dir, args.max_failures, ...) that are read this way and all of them could be directly obtained from the spec object?

I am using CliMT as a wrapper for RRTMG to calculate radiative fluxes and heatingrates. issue_title: [minor] Test helper fill_socket is probably unreliable on Windows
Portable way of waiting for sockets when all you have is a socket descriptor, not a socket object
issue_body:
I'm writing an async wrapper over psycopg2 for my ORM to work with trio, and I've hit a snag with it - as far as I can see in the docs, there's no universal way of waiting until a file descriptor is readable.

There's `trio.hazmat.wait_readable`, but that only works on *nix, and `wait_socket_readable` (as shown in the docs) only works on `socket.socket` objects. 

I took a quick search of the issues and couldn't find anything related to this either.

issue_title: Support for PEP 567
issue_body:
[PEP 567 got accepted](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0567/) on the 22nd, adding context-local variables to Python 3.7, and it would be super useful to have implemented (especially for something I'm working on).

However, 3.5/3.6 compat is an issue, and if it wasn't for that I would've PR'd in already (since the change is probably super trivial, `msg = task.coro.send(next_send)` -> `context = contextvars.copy_context(); msg = context.run(task.coro.send, next_send)` or similar.), and I don't know how that would be solved.

issue_title: URL resolver makes local copies of local copies
issue_body:
When processing files in workspaces, Resolver is responsible for making local copies of remote URLs. It does so by converting them to safe local path names.

But when the same input is used for multiple outputs, it gets re-copied, converting a local path to an even longer local path, which can quickly give 

> OSError: [Errno 36] File name too long:

issue_title: RecordLink.blocker and Gazetteer.blocker create a huge number of blocks
issue_body:
Both RecordLink.blocker and Gazetteer.blocker produce a huge number of blocks, which makes them practically unusable.

I use a dedupe version downloaded on May 2017 (not sure about the exact version number).
Python version is 3.5.3 and the server is RHEL 6.5 .

Some code for reproducing the problem is attached. It's based on pgsql_big_dedupe_example.
Please see the "instructions.txt" file for instructions.

[Blocker problem.zip](https://github.com/dedupeio/dedupe/files/1100245/Blocker.problem.zip)

It demonstrates how RecordLink.blocker generates over 200 affiliations to blocks per donor in average, 
 while Gazetteer.blocker generates even more affiliations by far.
Compared to that, Dedupe.blocker only generates about 1.5 affiliations to blocks per donor.

A few notes:

  1. They all use the same JSON training file, with about 50 "yes" and 50 "no" records. 

  2. Since pgsql_big_dedupe_example contains a single list of donors rather than two lists, the sampling is done by using the same list twice. 
     I know it doesn't make much sense to do record linking between two identical data sets, but I think it's still a valid example for testing the blocking. Originally, I encountered this problem using two different data sets.

  3. Dedupe.blocker.index_fields contains the "address" field, so it is indexed (using Dedupe.blocker.index).
     However, RecordLink.blocker.index_fields does not contain any fields (although the same JSON training file is used), so no indexing is done.
     Gazetteer.blocker.index_fields is different from both, containing only the "name" field.

issue_title: more precise structural summarization instead of NLP
issue_body:
@bogdanp05 , one idea that we should think about is the following:
- instead of summarizing stuff with NLP maybe we can take inspiration from the python profiler, which has the same task to achieve as the one we have: summarize multiple stack traces. 

the profiler, afaik, wakes up multiple times, observes the stack trace, and then it summarizes everything at the end with a view like the following one: 

![images duckduckgo](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/464519/39584275-50082fa2-4ef2-11e8-8fe8-ae7150ae8816.jpg)

maybe we could do something similar, since after all, we also have a bunch of stack traces that we want to summarize!

surely, the profiler estimates time spent in a given method, and we must summarize the number of times the outlier was found being in that method, while calling from the previous method, but it should be a similar thing. 

think about this, and let's discuss it the next time we meet!

issue_title: Automatically set password on Django side on successful LDAP authentication
issue_body:
According to the docs, an unusable password is set for any user created by the LDAPBackend. While I agree that this should be the default behavior, I think that we should also add an option that automatically sets/updates/mirrors the password of the user object on the Django side if the LDAP credentials are valid.

One scenario where this is especially useful is if one has absolutely no control/visibility over anything involving the LDAP server, like in the case of a 3rd party vendor integrating with a client's LDAP server. If for some reason the client's LDAP server is inaccessible, with this option, the existing users can still use the Django application with the same AD credentials w/ no additional steps from a sysadmin. At least this way, you have some form of graceful degradation.

Anyway, I took a quick look at the code, and I can think of two ways to add this option. Both options involve passing the password to the `_get_or_create_user` method of `_LDAPUser` as shown [here](https://github.com/django-auth-ldap/django-auth-ldap/blob/5c3fbdc9058bdaffb40127dd77d0bf692ecec379/django_auth_ldap/backend.py#L348-L350). From there, we can then create a new setting to run the automatic behavior. If it is more desirable from a design perspective to let the developers handle that explicitly, we can also opt to pass the password to the `django_auth_ldap.backend.populate_user` signal instead.

This is all assuming I got things right. If the password is already an attribute of another argument of the `populate_user` signal, then perhaps we can note that in the documentation. In any case, I will be glad to create a PR for this. So.. thoughts on this one?

issue_title: Namespaces beginning with _ are invalid
issue_body:
wwai...@gmail.com, 2010-11-26T22:53:44.000Z 
The NamespaceManager will invent namespace prefixes when its
compute_qname method is called. The prefixes it invents start with
an underscore. The legality of this might be debated but there are
several parsers, amongst them redland, that cannot read N3/turtle
generated by rdflib because of this unless inordinate care is taken
that all possible prefixes be defined beforehand.

The fix below copies the behaviour of Virtuoso which invents prefixes
starting with "ns" and a number.

Patch below

---

diff --git a/rdflib/namespace.py b/rdflib/namespace.py
index 935f1a1..127a854 100644
--- a/rdflib/namespace.py
+++ b/rdflib/namespace.py
@@ -191,7 +191,12 @@ class NamespaceManager(object):
             namespace = URIRef(namespace)
             prefix = self.store.prefix(namespace)
             if prefix is None:
-                prefix = "_%s" % len(list(self.store.namespaces()))
+                num = 1
+                while 1:
+                    prefix = "ns%s" % num
+                    if not self.store.namespace(prefix):
+                        break
+                    num += 1
                 self.bind(prefix, namespace)
             self.__cache[uri] = (prefix, namespace, name)
         return self.__cache[uri]
diff --git a/test/test_namespace.py b/test/test_namespace.py
new file mode 100644
index 0000000..4aba036
--- /dev/null
+++ b/test/test_namespace.py
@@ -0,0 +1,17 @@
+from rdflib.graph import Graph
+from rdflib.term import URIRef
+
+def test_qname():
+    """Test sequential assignment of unknown prefixes"""
+    g = Graph()
+    assert g.compute_qname(URIRef("http://foo/bar/baz")) == \
+        ("ns1", URIRef("http://foo/bar/"), "baz")
+
+    assert g.compute_qname(URIRef("http://foo/bar#baz")) == \
+        ("ns2", URIRef("http://foo/bar#"), "baz")
+    
+    # should skip this because we already assign it
+    g.bind("ns3", URIRef("http://exampe.org/"))
+
+    assert g.compute_qname(URIRef("http://blip/blop")) == \
+        ("ns4", URIRef("http://blip/"), "blop")

Comment 1 by ed.summers
Can you attach the output of `svn diff` to this ticket please? It will make applying the patch a lot easier.


Comment 2 by wwai...@gmail.com
As an attachment... It should apply with 'patch -p1 < qname_prefix.diff'


Comment 3 by ed.summers



Comment 4 by ed.summers



Comment 5 by ed.summers
I guess we can chalk this up to a difference between Turtle and N3. Turtle is a subset of N3. So what is valid N3 isn't necessarily valid Turtle. In this case N3 allows prefixes to start with a underscore, and Turtle does not. And redland understands Turtle, whereas rdflib understands N3 :-)

That being said I think it makes sense to encourage a bit more interoperability between redland and rdflib by making the change you suggest:


>>> g = rdflib.Graph()
>>> g.add((rdflib.URIRef('http://example.com/foo'), rdflib.URIRef('http://example.com/bar'), rdflib.Literal(1)))
>>> print g.serialize(format='n3')
@prefix _3:  .

 _3:bar 1 



will become:


>>> g = rdflib.Graph()
>>> g.add((rdflib.URIRef('http://example.com/foo'), rdflib.URIRef('http://example.com/bar'), rdflib.Literal(1)))
>>> g.serialize(format='n3')
'@prefix ns1:  .\n@prefix ns2:  .\n\nns1:foo ns1:bar 1 .\n\n'
>>> print g.serialize(format='n3')
@prefix ns1:  .
@prefix ns2:  .

ns1:foo ns1:bar 1 .



It's interesting that the subject and object URIs becomes prefixed after this change. I wonder if that could potentially end up blowing up the number of prefixes used when serializing as N3?


Comment 6 by ed.summers
I'm not sure this "blowing up" of prefixes demonstrated in test.py (attached) is good for large graphs:

before patch:

% ./test.py

 a  .

 a  .

 a  .

 a  .

 a  .

 a  .

 a  .

 a  .

 a  .


after patch:

% ./test.py 
@prefix ns1:  .
@prefix ns10:  .
@prefix ns2:  .
@prefix ns3:  .
@prefix ns4:  .
@prefix ns5:  .
@prefix ns6:  .
@prefix ns7:  .
@prefix ns8:  .
@prefix ns9:  .

ns6:i a ns2:Person .

ns8:i a ns2:Person .

ns4:i a ns2:Person .

ns3:i a ns2:Person .

ns1:i a ns2:Person .

ns5:i a ns2:Person .

ns7:i a ns2:Person .

ns9:i a ns2:Person .

ns10:i a ns2:Person .


Comment 7 by wwai...@gmail.com
attached patch modifies the turtle serialiser to prevent prefix explosion...


Comment 9 by ed.summers
I'm just realizing now that the Turtle serializer emits the prefix with a leading underscore...so this really is a defect.


Comment 10 by ed.summers
This issue was closed by revision r1899.


Comment 11 by ed.summers
This issue was closed by revision dcea99e96edc.

issue_title: Nest expectations on return values
issue_body:
In python-mock library, `return_value` can be used to chain expectations (as it is also a mock), example:

```python
    def test_mongo_db_api_connects_to_mongo(self):
        with mock.patch('pymongo.Connection') as mock_conn:
            stub_cursor = "whatever"
            mock_conn.return_value.__getitem__ = mock.MagicMock()
            mock_conn.return_value.__getitem__.return_value.__getitem__.return_value = stub_cursor

            api = MongoDbApi(database_name='dbname', collection_name='collection')
            self.assertEquals(api.cursor, stub_cursor)

            mock_conn.assert_called_with({'host':'localhost'})
            mock_conn.return_value.__getitem__.assert_called_with('dbname')
            mock_conn.return_value.__getitem__.return_value.__getitem__.assert_called_with('collection')
```

would be nice if something similar was available in chai, maybe like this:

```python
    def test_mongo_db_api_connects_to_mongo(self):
        stub_cursor = stub("whatever")
        expect(pymongo.Connection).args({'host':'localhost'}).\
                return_stub.expect('__getitem__').args('dbname').\
                return_stub.expect('__getitem__').args('collection').\
                returns( stub_cursor )
        api = MongoDbApi(database_name='dbname', collection_name='collection')
        assert_equals(api.cursor, stub_cursor)
```

It does behave a bit different though, since the object that receives the method now has an .expect() method which is different from how Chai usually sets expectations on objects, but on other hand it looks so cool! :)

issue_title: pyzmq hard locks Windows 7 with fast socket open/close
issue_body:
Found this out the hard way with some buggy code that used to work "fine" on a Windows XP box.

This is probably a libzmq issue, and not specifically a pyzmq issue, but I have never written any C which uses libzmq, so am not sure how to recreate it. I thought I would post it here for anyone who encounters the issue.

While the code below is not ideal, (like I said, it was found in something buggy), it seems it shouldn't be able to hard lock the machine. I have been able to lock up the client with a sleep of one to five seconds between sockets occasionally as well, though inconsistently.

## Example Server
```python
import zmq

def run():
    ctx = zmq.Context()

    sock = ctx.socket(zmq.REP)
    sock.bind('tcp://127.0.0.1:5555')

    print "Waiting..."
    while True:
       msg = sock.recv()
       sock.send('10-4')
       print msg

if __name__ == '__main__':
    run()
```

## Example Client
```python
import zmq

def connect_and_send(ctx, msg):
    sock = ctx.socket(zmq.REQ)
    sock.connect('tcp://127.0.0.1:5555')
    sock.send(msg)
    sock.recv()
    sock.close()

def run():
    ctx = zmq.Context()

    count = 1
    while True:
        connect_and_send(ctx, "Msg %s" % count)
        count += 1

if __name__ == '__main__':
    run()
```
